results.summary: FACTS: The CTM holder registered the word mark COLOURBLIND as a CTM in respect of a range of goods and services in Classes 9, 16, 28, 35 and 41. The invalidity applicant sought the cancellation of the contested CTM on the grounds of Article 52 (1) (b) and Article 53 (1) (c), read in conjunction with Article 8 (4) CTMR. The Cancellation Division (CD) upheld the application for a declaration of invalidity, finding that bad faith had been established on the basis of Article 52 (1) (b) CTMR, and declared the contested trade mark to be invalid. On appeal, the Fourth Board of Appeal (BoA) annulled the CD’s decision and dismissed the application for a declaration of invalidity in its entirety. SUBSTANCE: The General Court (GC) dismissed the appeal and confirmed the BoA’s findings. Concerning the ‘passing off’ claim based on Article 53 (1) (c), read in conjunction with Article 8 (4) CTMR, the GC noted that the invalidity applicant had only filed two invoices, each of which concerned the sale of seven units of a learning product distinguished by the contested CTM, and that this could not prove a sufficiently significant use of the sign ‘COLOURBLIND’ in the course of trade by the invalidity applicant, so that the claim was dismissed. Concerning the bad faith claim based on Article 52 (1) (b) CTMR, the GC noted that it was not disputed by the parties that the ‘COLOURBLIND’ product consisting of an ‘“experimental learning” toolbox’ was invented in 1991 by a physical person, a Mr C.  (main shareholder and director of the CTM holder at the time of the invalidity request), who in 1993 had set up the invalidity applicant’s company, Mr C. holding 99% of the latter’s shares. Although Mr. C. was closely and continuously involved, from 1991 until 2003, in activities leading to the use of ‘COLOURBLIND’ by the invalidity applicant first and later by another connected company, and was therefore necessarily aware of that use, such awareness on his part - noted by the GC - was not sufficient, in itself, to establish that the CTM holder (whom Mr. C. controlled) acted in bad faith. The GC dismissed the invalidity applicant’s argument that Mr C.’s aim in filing the application for registration of the contested trade mark was to ‘lay his hands’ on the trade mark of a third party which he had had contractual or pre-contractual relations with. In that respect, the GC held that, owing to the connections between Mr C. and the invalidity applicant (the former holding in the early 90’s 99% shares of the latter), the invalidity applicant could not be described as a third party in relation to Mr C. The GC also found that the main item of evidence relied upon by the invalidity applicant (a share purchase agreement) in order to argue that the rights in the contested CTM had been assigned to it by Mr. C. only contained a generic reference to ‘warranties’ and ‘intellectual property’ and that there was no mention of ‘COLOURBLIND’ in that agreement. The GC went on to note that, even assuming it to be established that the sign ‘COLOURBLIND’ was indeed covered by that agreement, all that was mentioned with regard to the invalidity applicant was its being the beneficial owner of rights in that sign, not that it had legal title to those rights The GC went on to say  that, if there was an agreement between Mr C. and the invalidity applicant concerning the use of ‘COLOURBLIND’, it is possible that, owing to its implied or informal nature, it was not mentioned in the mentioned share purchase agreement. The GC held that, in the light of the evidence produced, it could not be excluded that Mr C. considered himself to have rights in the sign ‘COLOURBLIND’ up until the moment of its transfer to the CTM holder and that both the invalidity applicant and a third, connected company that had later made use of the sign were acting under an implied or informal agreement on the part of Mr C.

results.summary: The decision deals with the question whether a famous castle, which is also a tourist attraction, is descriptive of the geographical origin of the products covered by the sign or devoid of any distinctiveness. In the Boards opinion, the sign is apt for registration. Even if some consumers perceived the sign as referring also (but not exclusively) to the famous castle it could still fulfil its function as a badge of commercial origin.

The appeal is proposed by three invalidity applicants and it is declared inadmissible because two of them did not file the NOAP in due time. The proceedings continue in the merits only respect one of them.
The invalidity applicant failed to prove to be the owner of the earlier unregistered trade marks, so that the claim under Article 8(3) CTMR fails. He also failed to prove  the bad faith of the CTM proprietor. It is certain that there was a commercial relationship between the parties, but it is not demonstrated what kind of relationship it was and, therefore, whether or not was fair by part of the CTM proprietor to file the trade mark at issue.

results.summary:
FACTS:  The applicant sought the declaration of invalidity of the CTM VOODOO (word) registered for several goods in class 25 based on Article 52 (1) (a) in conjunction with Article 7 (1) (b) and (c) CTMR and on Article 52 (1) (b) CTMR. The First Instance rejected the request for declaration of invalidity. The Board of Appeal (BoA) upheld that decision.  SUBSTANCE: Descriptiveness and lack of distinctive character. The applicant contends that the term “VOODOO” in connection with goods in class 25 would describe a specific style of clothing or footwear which is used in the Voodoo-religion. Consumers would immediately understand this relation and consider the registered mark as descriptive. The General Court (GC) confirms the conclusions in the contested decision that the evidence submitted by the applicant does not show that it is reasonable to believe that it will actually be recognised by the relevant public as a description of one of the characteristics of the goods (para. 32). The applicant could not show that the BoA was wrong in concluding that the mark will be seen as a fantasy term which only has a vague reference to something occult or cultic (para. 33). Consequently, the mark may not be considered as directly descriptive term nor does it constitute a laudatory message or an abstract indication of quality of the goods (para. 51). 

Burden to show the applicability of the invoked absolute grounds. The applicant claimed that the BoA infringed Article 76 CTMR by saying that the applicant would have to show the applicability of the invoked absolute grounds for refusal. The GC interpreted this plea in the sense that the BoA had not taken into consideration some of the evidence put forward by the applicant and that it did don’t undertake some further investigation (para. 39). The GC concludes that the BoA did take into account all the evidence submitted during the administrative procedure as well as the information which may be generally accessed (para. 41). Consequently, there is no infringement of Article 76 CTMR (para. 42). 

Bad Faith (Article 52 (1) (b) CTMR). The first argument put forward by the applicant that the CTM proprietor knew that the applied for mark would lack distinctive character, because the German Trademark Office (DPMA) refused registration of the same mark has been rejected by the GC. The refusal decision from the German Trademark Office has been notified after the relevant date for the assessment of bad faith, which is the date of filing (para. 58). The applicant further contends that the intervener was in bad faith because it claimed also a priority based on the German mark which has been found not distinctive by the German Trademark Office. The GC refused that argument because a priority may be lawfully claimed from an application being the subsequent outcome of that application irrelevant (see Article 29 (3) CTMR) (para. 59). Finally, the applicant inferred bad faith from the fact that the Intervener tried to circumvent the obligation of use of its prior CTM registration “VOODOO” for class 25 (No. 1 911 742). It is interesting that the GC upheld in this respect the argument of the Office that there would be no bad faith, since the earlier CTM is registered for the class headings whereas the later CTM has been registered for specific goods in class 25. Therefore the Intervener applied for the second mark for the sake of clearness and preciseness of the scope of protection of its “VOODOO” mark and not in a fraudulent way (para. 60).

results.summary:
FACTS: This case concerns a situation where the Romanian collective trade mark URB were once exploited by different producers of bearings, under the control of a public entity linked to the Romanian Government and which also owned those collective marks. Following the start of a privatisation process, the assets of this public entity were transferred to a private company which became the registered owner. However, several companies, including the invalidity applicant and another company of which the CTM proprietor, a physical person, was the manager and shareholder, were authorised to use the earlier collective trademarks. In 2002, following an amendment, the use regulation did no longer contain the list of undertakings authorised to use those collective trademarks. It was rather established that third parties would have the right to use them only under a franchise agreement managed by the registered proprietor of those trademarks, but the invalidity applicant never entered such an agreement.
Following registration of the contested mark URB by the CTM proprietor, the invalidity applicant sought its cancellation, relying, on the one hand, on two earlier registered Romanian collective trademarks and on two International trademark registrations designating a number of Member States and, on the other, of the alleged bad faith of the CTM proprietor.
The evidence showed that these four registrations stood in the name of a third company, The  invalidity applicant asserted that it was authorised to use those trademarks and to file the invalidity action, as the registered owner of those trademarks, expressly requested, had declined to do so.  
The Cancellation Division rejected the invalidity request on the grounds that no evidence had been adduced of the bad faith of the CTM proprietor and that the invalidity applicant, which was not the proprietor of the earlier marks, was not entitled to rely on them to argue the existence of a likelihood of confusion. On appeal, the Board confirmed the first instance’s decision.
SUBSTANCE:  The General court (GC) dismissed the appeal. It was not persuaded by the invalidity applicant’s argument that, given the refusal to act of the registered owner of the earlier marks, it was entitled, pursuant to the provisions of Article 22(2) and (3) and of Article 72 CTMR, to bring invalidity proceedings. 
In first place, the GC noted that under Article 56(1)(b) CTMR, an application for a declaration of invalidity  may be submitted to the Office, where Article 53(1) applies, by the proprietors of earlier trademarks and licensees authorised by the proprietors of those trade marks (para. 17). It did not appear from the documents before the GC that the invalidity applicant either had a licence or, in any event, had been authorised by the registered proprietor of the earlier marks to file an application for a declaration of invalidity with the Office (para. 19). 
Secondly, the GC noted that the provisions of Articles 22(3) and 72 CTMR are not applicable to invalidity requests but only to actions for infringement, and that the same was true of the provisions of Romanian law relied on by the invalidity applicant (para. 20).
As to the alleged bad faith, the GC referred to the existing Case-Law and recalled that account may also be taken of the commercial logic underlying the filing of the application for registration of a Community trade mark. Even in circumstances where several producers were using, on the market identical or similar signs for identical or similar products, capable of being confused with the sign for which registration was sought, still the CTM proprietor’s registration of the sign may be in pursuit of a legitimate objective (para. 35). 
The GC held that it was understandable, from a commercial point of view that the CTM proprietor wished to extend the protection of the URB trade mark by registering it as a Community trade mark. In that respect, the evidence on file showed that, during the period which preceded the filing, the CTM proprietor used the trademark, through a connected company, and generated turnover from goods marketed under the trademark in several Member States, which was a plausible incentive for filing an application for the registration of a Community trade mark (para. 41). 
The invalidity applicant did not establish that it was the CTM proprietor’s intention to exclude it from the market (para. 44). The fact that the CTM proprietor knew or should have known that a third party (i.e. the invalidity applicant in this case) has long been using, in at least one Member State, an identical or similar sign for an identical or similar product capable of being confused with the sign for which registration is sought is not sufficient, in itself, to permit the conclusion that the CTM proprietor was acting in bad faith when filing the application (para. 47).

results.summary:
FACTS: An application for invalidity was filed against the figurative CTM represented below and registered for goods in classes 9, 14 and services in class 35, based on Articles 52(1)(b) and 53(1)(a) CTMR (bad faith filing and relative grounds for invalidity (likelihood of confusion, unfair advantage/detriment). The Cancellation Division rejected the request, stating that the application for invalidity was inadmissible according to Article 165(4) (b) insofar as it was based on Czech and Slovak earlier trade marks. Regarding the invoked earlier international trade mark, the submitted evidence was not enough for proving genuine use. Bad faith was not proven in the proceedings either; mere knowledge of the earlier marks is not sufficient, in itself, to conclude that the applicant was acting in bad faith. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the applicants’ appeal. The applicants filed an action before the General court (GC).
 
SUBSTANCE: the GC rejected the action as partially manifestly inadmissible and partially manifestly lacking any foundation in law. The action was found to be unclear and poorly structured (par 22); the GC nevertheless identified six pleas in law: (1) breach of Article 8(1)(a)(b) CTMR; (2) breach of Article 8(5)CTMR; (3) breach of Article 54 and 165(4) CTMR; (4) breach of Article 56 and 41 CTMR; (5) incorrect assessment of evidence of use of earlier international registration; (6) breach of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The first two pleas in law were rejected as irrelevant, because the applicants requested the Court to carry out a substantial assessment of Articles 8(1) and (5), which was not carried out by the BoA. The third plea in law was found to be manifestly lacking any foundation in law. A CTM cannot be declared invalid if the earlier national right was registered, applied for or acquired in a new Member State prior to the date of accession (Art 165(4) CTMR). Article 54 invoked by the applicants was not at all mentioned in the BoA’s decision. The fourth plea in law is manifestly inadmissible. The arguments of the applicants stating that the assertions of the Office regarding joint ownership of the earlier marks are “totally irrelevant and unfounded” are not sufficiently clear and precise to enable the defendant to prepare its defence and the GC to exercise its power of judicial review. Regarding the fifth plea, the part of applicants’ argumentation where they state that the Office “essentially erred in law” as it did not attribute sufficient weight to the evidence provided (one affidavit), is considered manifestly inadmissible, as it is too general to enable the defendant to prepare its defence and the GC to exercise its power of judicial review. The applicants’ argument that the submitted affidavit was “incorrectly assessed by the Office” is considered manifestly unfounded; it is evident that the BoA duly examined the affidavit in question before concluding that it was not enough to prove use of the earlier international registration. The sixth plea is partially manifestly inadmissible and partially manifestly unfounded. Firstly, the applicants’ arguments are very short, unclear and imprecise. The applicants criticise the Office for not having taken into account the evidence submitted by them and for not having given appropriate weight to such evidence. This criticism is again too vague and hence manifestly inadmissible. The arguments regarding previous knowledge of the applicants’ marks by the CTM proprietor are manifestly unfounded. Firstly, this fact became clear during the proceedings and secondly, mere knowledge is not sufficient, in itself, to conclude that the applicant was acting in bad faith. The applicants’ assertions that the BoA did not deal with the issue of licence agreements between the parties is erroneous, because the BoA dealt with this issue in paragraph 41 of the contested decision. Regarding the inclusion of numeral 1949 in the contested CTM, supposedly giving a wrong impression about the company's history and hence inserted in bad faith, the GC stated that mere insertion of a year in the contested CTM cannot be on its own conclusive about any bad faith on the part of the CTM proprietor. This argument is manifestly unfounded.

results.summary:
On 12 May 2004, the CTM owner applied to register the word mark ‘GRUPPO SALINI’ as a CTM in respect of services in classes 36, 37 and 42. The registration was granted on 12 September 2005. On 5 October 2007, the invalidity applicant filed an invalidity request, based, inter alia, on Article 52(1)(b) CTMR on the one hand and on Article 53(1)(c) CTMR on the other. The invalidity applicant claimed that the CTM owner was in bad faith when applying to register the contested mark.  

The Cancellation Division rejected the invalidity request. 

On appeal, the Board annulled the decision of the first instance. It considered, inter alia, that the invalidity applicant had proved to the requisite legal standard that the CTM owner was in bad faith at the time of filing.

The CTM owner appealed the decision before the GC, who dismissed the appeal. 

SUBSTANCE: 
The GC found that the CTM owner could not ignore, and was probably aware that, the invalidity applicant had been using the sign SALINI (alone or in combination with the word ‘COSTRUTTORI’ (meaning ‘builders’) both in Italy and abroad for a long time in order to distinguish its activity in respect of building activities and the planning of public infrastructures. 
Moreover, the directors of the CTM owner were not only members of the same enlarged Salini family, but they were sitting on the board of directors of the invalidity applicant (one of them having acted as President of the board of directors between 2000 and 2003) and were therefore in a position to influence the business strategies of the invalidity applicant, including the choice of applying to register the SALINI trade mark, that had been in use for several decades (para. 25). 
The GC then focused on the timing of events and noted that the CTM owner had filed the contested mark only a few months after the start of the pending (before the Italian Courts) dispute between the parties and had taken advantage of the situation of legal uncertainty concerning the internal balance of the invalidity applicant, and that the filing had taken place during a phase of rapid growth, both domestically and internationally, of the invalidity applicant. The GC thus held that the CTM owner could not possibly ignore the potential damage that its initiative of filing the contested mark implied for the invalidity applicant’s business strategies (para. 30). 
The GC then noted that the fact that the invalidity applicant had not registered its brand ‘SALINI’ could be explained with its belief that its prior use of the sign conferred onto it legal ownership rights under Italian law and, in particular, with the invalidity applicant’s willingness to respect the ‘gentlemen agreement’ that was apparently in force between the various members of the Salini family and which meant that they could use the name Salini to distinguish their different business activities, but not attempt to register it (para. 31). 
The GC further noted that the invalidity applicant had adopted, back in 2003, an ethical code where reference was made to ‘Gruppo Salini’ and it was explained that said code was binding for all the members of the group of companies (i.e. including the CTM owner) and that the expression ‘Gruppo Salini’ designated the invalidity applicant and any other company controlled by it (i.e. also the CTM owner). Thus, the CTM owner cannot reasonably claim that, at the time of filing, it ignored the existence of the mentioned ethical code and the definition of the expression ‘Gruppo Salini’ therein contained (para. 32).
Finally, the GC confirmed the Board’s finding that the existence of bad faith at the time of filing entails the invalidity of the contested mark in respect of all of the services covered by it Having found that the Board had correctly concluded that the invalidity applicant had established that the CTM owner was in bad faith at the time of filing, the GC refrained from addressing the other grounds and pleas on which the appeal was based.(para. 48).

results.summary:
FACTS: A request for a declaration of invalidity was filed against the contested CTM registration on the basis of an absolute ground, namely that the CTM proprietor was acting in bad faith when filing the application for the contested CTM registration, within the meaning of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The request for a declaration of invalidity was directed against the services in Classes 35 and 39. The Cancellation Division and the Board of Appeal dismissed the request.
SUBSTANCE: The General Court found that there was no positive evidence that the CTM application was made in bad faith in respect of services in Classes 35 and 39, even if such services exceeded the scope of the applicant’s field of business on the date of filing, and even if this application followed the revocation of an almost identical CTM registration on account of non-use in respect of services belonging to the same classes. 
The General Court found that where the proprietor of a CTM makes repeated applications for the same mark with the effect of avoiding the consequences of revocation for non-use of earlier CTMs, whether in whole or in part, this can be held as an indication that the proprietor is acting in bad faith. The chronology of events may also be a relevant factor (Para. 27). 
However, the application for an updated version of the revoked CTM registration may be justified by legitimate considerations such as the intention to adapt to a new corporate identity by registering a modernised trade mark covering an updated list of services (Paras. 35-36), or the desire to merge the lists of a number of existing CTMs in order to avoid multiple renewals (Paras. 37-38). 
Neither the CTMR nor the case-law provides a basis for finding that there is bad faith in view of the size of the list of goods and services in the application for registration (Paras. 54-55).

results.quotation: (31/05/2012, R 2055/2010‑1, WHITE BOX WITH A CHEQUERED LID (3D))
results.nickName: WHITE BOX WITH A CHEQUERED LID (3D)
results.summary: 1.	The chequered device is a basic pattern which has been widely used over the ages in relation to food products, including the goods concerned in Classes 18, 29, 30 and 32. The device is, in other words, a usual and commonplace decorative feature of many food products. The contested sign displays no elements or noticeable variation, in particular in terms of fancifulness or as regards the way in which its components are combined, which would distinguish it from the usual representation of a chequered tablecloth which is characterized by a regular pattern of squares in alternating colours, in dark and bright shades so as to endow a trade mark with any distinctive character.  the sign contains nothing which would distinguish it from other (‘chequered) designs traditionally used in the sector and which could, ab initio, confer a distinctive character to the contested sign overall.2.	The CTM proprietor submitted the following material to the CD:  a survey conducted in December 2003 in France, an advertisement booklet dated 1971, an advertisement dated 1973; figures for advertising campaigns in France from 1978 to 1988, figures about marketing in France, Italy and the United Kingdom, articles from French magazines, a judgment from the Paris Court of Appeal. In addition, the customer survey only concerns France whereas the mark has been registered as a CTM and therefore as such for the EU. The CTM proprietor has not adduced sufficient evidence with regard to the use of the contested mark in all MSs. Furthermore, no inference as regards the perception of the relevant public could be made from the list of registered marks, nor from the sales and turnover figures given the absence of a clear indication as to the mark and the goods concerned. Thus, based on the elements submitted before the CD, it was not established that the contested CTM had acquired distinctive character in, for example, Finland, Portugal, Sweden and Denmark as well as in Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Malta, Cyprus, Poland, Slovenia, the Slovak Republic and Bulgaria, through the use made of it neither at its filling date nor after its registration.3.	The appeal is dismissed.

keywords: Restriction of the list of goods and services
results.summary: 1.	A far as the cancellation applicants argument is concerned, which states that the CTM proprietor has no interest in selling specific goods through retailing, wholesaling or sale by mail order as it is a pharmaceutical wholesaler, it should be noted that this is not sufficient for the assumption of conduct in bad faith on the date of the application. The Board cannot discern that the CTM proprietor exclusively intended to impede the cancellation applicant when registering its trade mark. Further arguments and evidence would have had to be submitted for this; however, these have not been provided by the cancellation applicant. European trade mark law does not stipulate that a trade mark can only be registered if a type of use already exists. Rather, European trade mark law allows the trade mark proprietor to include types of use up to 5 years after registration. And even if there is no genuine use, this does not constitute proof of possible conduct in bad faith on the part of the proprietor.2.	The appeal is dismissed.

results.quotation: (03/05/2012, R 1052/2010‑1, EIFFEL / Eiffel GESTIÓN HIPOTECARIA (fig.) et al.)
results.nickName: EIFFEL / Eiffel GESTIÓN HIPOTECARIA (fig.) et al.
keywords: Dissimilarity of the goods and services

results.summary: 1. Since the contested decision adversely affects the applicant only in so far as the opposition was upheld, the appellant has no interest in appealing the contested decision for those goods and services for which the opposition was rejected. Nevertheless, it is apparent from the arguments set out in their observations that the opponents challenge the conclusion of the OD with respect to the goods designated by the contested mark which were found dissimilar to the earlier services. 2. This amounts to an ancillary appeal. 3. As a result of the appeal filed by both parties, the contested decision has been appealed in its entirety. 4. The Board endorses the contested decisions findings regarding the alleged substantiation shortcomings and the alleged missing name of the second opponent in the opposition notice. 5. As regards the seniority claim and the claim of ‘bad faith, they are not relevant as regards this opposition. Moreover, the fact of claiming seniority of an earlier (Spanish) mark for the CTM application would not change the date of the CTM application and convert it into a mark which would be prior to the opponents marks. 6. As for the claim of bad faith with respect to the use of the word ‘eiffel, it is not the Offices nor the Boards task, in the context of the opposition proceedings, to rule on the misleading or deceptive character of the earlier marks or on the alleged bad faith in the registration of the earlier marks. 7. As no proof of pending national Spanish proceeding which could affect the validity of the earlier rights have been submitted, the applicants argument in that respect are rejected. 8. As to the third partys observations submitted pursuant to Article 40 CTMR, the arguments based on Article 7(1)(g) and Article 52(1)(b) cannot form a basis for the opposition since an opposition can only be filed on the grounds set out in Article 8 CTMR. 9. The contested sign, which consists of the unique word ‘eiffel is identical to the dominant element of the earlier signs, except for the slight stylization of the words ‘eiffel in the earlier signs. 10. This causes the signs to be visually similar and aurally highly similar, despite the additional features of the earlier signs which have a limited visual impact on the public. 11. There is a conceptual similarity between the signs which derives from the common word ‘eiffel, confirmed by the figurative device in the earlier signs and which is not counterbalanced by the words ‘GESTIÓN HIPOTECARIA in the earlier signs. 12. The Board concludes that the signs are similar overall despite their differences. 13. Bearing in mind that the word ‘eiffel is distinctive, at least to a certain degree, in relation to the G&S at issue, the fact that the level of attention is higher than average is not sufficient to exclude the possibility of LOC as regards those G&S which have been found to be identical and similar in Classes 6, 7, 12, 19, 36, 37, 39, 40  and 42. 14. However, there cannot be LOC with respect to the G&S which have been found dissimilar to the services designated by the earlier marks (see paragraph 78 of the decision). 15. The contested decision is partly annulled.

results.summary: 1.	The fact that the trade mark proprietor knew of the existence of the earlier trade marks of the applicant for a declaration of invalidity and nevertheless filed the application for the CTM constitutes evidence of obstructive intent and his bad faith. In this respect, it is irrelevant that he assumed that these trade marks were ready for cancellation. The Board considers this to be merely a pretext since the proprietor has still not filed an application for revocation of the French trade mark or the international registration. The trade mark proprietor was also very well aware that the trade mark ‘SIMCA has become renowned in relation to motor vehicles. The evidence, primarily that submitted by the trade mark proprietor, shows that he was seeking a well-known trade mark name. Therefore, his intention was to free-ride on the coat-tails of the applicants registered trade marks having reputation, by intentionally using the sign ‘SIMCA without justification, and to trade upon the reputation of those trade marks. Such behaviour must be regarded as being in bad faith.2.	The Board annuls the contested decision and declares invalid CTM No 6 489 371.

results.summary:
FACTS: The CTM owner filed an application in 2005 for a figurative mark for ‘zip fasteners’ in Class 26; registration was issued in 2006. An application for invalidity due to bad faith based on an earlier Taiwanese figurative mark for ‘zip fasteners’ in Class 26 was rejected by the Cancellation Division. An appeal was filed and the Board of Appeal dismissed the appeal, finding that the evidence before it was insufficient to establish bad faith on the part of the CTM owner. The application for annulment of the contested decision was upheld by the GC. 
SUBSTANCE: The GC upheld the Office’s claim that certain documents presented for the first time before the GC, and which were not included in the Office’s file, were inadmissible (Para. 26). The invalidity applicant’s claim for the GC to declare the CTM invalid was admissible (alteration according to Article 65(3) CTMR). However, the GC rejected the claim as the issues for which the Court annulled the Board’s decision did not in themselves enable a finding in favour of bad faith (Para. 54 et seq).

Analysing the invalidity applicant’s arguments and the six considerations taken into account by the Board of Appeal to come to the conclusion that the CTM owner had not acted in bad faith, the GC found that four of the considerations were not based on an analysis of all of the relevant factors and were not substantiated by the evidence in the file or were based on incorrect factual findings (Para. 50). First, the GC found that the Board did not make an examination of the marks based on their overall impression (Para. 40). Second, the GC considered that the Board’s finding that the invalidity applicant manufactured ‘zip fasteners’ according to the CTM owner’s instructions was not substantiated by evidence in the file (Para. 44). The GC noted that the CTM owner failed to produce evidence in this respect when asked to do so by the Court (Para. 43). Third, the GC found that the actual use of the CTM, which is relevant to the assessment of the CTM owner’s intention, was not substantiated by evidence in the file (Para. 47). Fourth, the GC found that the invalidity applicant had sufficiently supported its claim that it sold ‘zip fasteners’ in the EU (namely Poland) prior to the filing of the CTM, contrary to the finding in the contested decision. That finding is relevant to the assessment of the commercial efforts of the invalidity applicant and the CTM owner in the EU and, consequently, to the CTM owner’s intention (Para. 49). The two remaining of the Board’s considerations were considered insufficient on their own to establish whether the CTM owner had acted in bad faith (Para. 50-51).

1. Pursuant to Rule 15(1) which applies mutatis mutandis to cancellation requests based on relative grounds, the earlier rights must all belong to the same proprietor. This is not the case here as regards the earlier Czech and Slovak marks. Some marks can be invoked by Cancellation applicant 1, others by Cancellation applicant 2, but none of them by both jointly. The remedy procedure to be followed in cases of inadmissibility of the request was not followed by the CD. The Board cannot separate the cancellation proceedings into two sets, all the less as absolute grounds for invalidity are also invoked. 2. Moreover, none of the Czech and Slovak marks qualify as earlier rights. Pursuant to Article 164 (5)(b) CTMR, the contested CTM, filed prior to the enlargement of the EC to the Czech Republic and the Slovak Republic on 1 May 2004, is immune against cancellation requests or oppositions based on Czech or Slovak marks. The contested decision dealt with this as an admissibility issue for the cancellation request. That cannot be followed. The consequence of Article 165(4) CTMR is that such marks pre-existing in the Czech Republic or the Slovak Republic are not ‘earlier marks within the meaning of Article 8(2) and 165 CTMR. When the contested CTM was filed in 2003, it was filed for protection in the EC 15 and no conflict with Czech or Slovak marks existed. When the CTM application was extended to the Czech Republic and the Slovak Republic with effect of 1 May 2004, the pre-existing Czech or Slovak marks continue to give the right to prohibit use of the CTM in those MS under Article 165(5), but do not give the right to cancel the CTM with Community-wide effect, pursuant to Article 165(4) CTMR. 3.  The reliance on the international registration No 192 567 is unfounded for three reasons, first, because of the lack of legal standing of Cancellation applicant 2, second, due to the failure to translate the registration certificate, third, due to the failure to prove its genuine use. 4. The mere knowledge of the existence of trade marks rights of competitors does not constitute bad faith. The Cancellation applicants essentially rely on this argument, and all their evidence is meant to demonstrate the existence of such prior knowledge. What matters in the end is that it cannot constitute bad faith if the CTM proprietor now continues to produce clocks as one of the companies emerging from a privatisation process under Czech national law, supposedly in some form of coexistence with the Cancellation applicants 1 or 2, supposedly with some details of that process still being litigated today, but with the genuine intention to market its own goods under its own mark, and not with the sole aim of obstructing a competitor. 5. Whether or not there was a licence agreement binding the CTM proprietor, any such licence was related to the Czech marks held by one or the other Cancellation applicant. After the expiry of a licence agreement the ex-licensee is in no better, but in no worse situation than any third party. 6. As regards national court decisions, the Cancellation applicants at no point in time alleged that one of those decisions or judgments condemned the CTM proprietor for bad faith. 7. The appeal is dismissed.

results.summary: 1. The sign consists of a coloured pin, the tip of which is missing, and is supported by a rectangular base. 2. The contested decision erred in defining the trade mark as a view of a wind turbine tower bearing green stripes, and its entire reasoning is based on a mark which is not the contested mark. 3. The registration exactly specifies a shape element presented in 6 different colours. It does not appear from the scope of protection of the contested mark that it prevents the use of colours on towers but rather prevents the use of the specific sign, which is a coloured trapezium, or a confusingly similar one. 4. The Board cannot see why a mark consisting of a coloured pin cut off on the top and standing on a rectangle should be common and non distinctive for wind converters or parts thereof. Consequently, the request for a declaration of invalidity is not well-founded insofar as it is based on Article 7(1)(b) CTMR. 5. It is also rejected insofar as it is based on Article 7(1)(c) since the sign does not consist of the image of a wind turbine tower, but of a coloured trapezium. 6. For similar reasons, Article 7(1)(e)(ii) CTMR is not met either, as the CTM does not consist exclusively of the shape of the goods which is necessary to obtain a technical result. 7. There is no indication of bad faith either since the submissions of the opponent as well as the contested decision are based on a wrong perception of the type and scope of protection of the mark. 8. The contested decision is annulled and the request for a declaration of invalidity is rejected.

results.summary: 1.	The cancellation applicant has not proven that its mark had been put to genuine use in relation to the goods in Classes 9 and 14 within the relevant period and territory. Thus, the application for a declaration of invalidity shall therefore be rejected.2.	The cancellation applicant does not contest the finding of the CD that all the goods covered by the other rights are dissimilar to the goods covered by the contested trade mark. The cancellation applicant however submits that the issue of the likelihood of confusion must ‘be examined even if the goods would be dissimilar (sic). This position cannot be sustained.3.	In the present case, the cancellation applicant merely contends that detriment or unfair advantage would be a necessary consequence resulting automatically from the use of the sign applied for, in view of the strong reputation of its mark. In the same vein, the cancellation applicant also failed to provide any explanation concerning the detriment to the repute of its earlier marks. Therefore, the request for a declaration of invalidity has also to be rejected.4.	Since the cancellation applicant has not adduced any additional evidence or arguments intended to demonstrate bad faith on part of the CTM proprietor, the invoked declaration of invalidity is rejected. Indeed good faith being presumed, it is incumbent upon the cancellation applicant to prove or to provide arguments to the contrary.5.	The appeal is dismissed.

results.summary:
FACTS: The invalidity applicant sought to cancel CTM No 4363842 BIGAB alleging that its owner was acting in bad faith at the time of filing the CTMA, as its sole objective was to prevent the invalidity applicant from continuing to use its Benelux non-registered mark BIGA for similar products. The CTM was registered on 20 March 2006, but BIGAB has been used in Sweden and other countries since 1991, whereas the invalidity applicant did not start using the sign BIGA (in Benelux countries) until 1996. The Board confirmed the rejection of the request for invalidity. The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court.

SUBSTANCE:  The GC followed in essence the argumentation of the Board´s decision and confirmed the jurisprudence in relation to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR (bad faith invalidity ground) as it was interpreted by Case C-529/07 Chocoladefabriken Lindt &Sprüngli [2009] ECR I-4893 (Lindt Goldhase case). The BIGAB Judgment has clarified some other points which have to be taken into account in order to assess whether the applicant of a CTM is acting in bad faith at the time of filing the application.

The GC clarified that the three factors set out in the Lindt Goldhase Judgment that is, i) knowledge by the applicant that a third party is using a sign which could give rise to confusion with the sign for which registration is sought, ii) applicant´s intention of preventing that third party from using such a sign and iii) degree of legal protection enjoyed by the third party´s sign and by the sign for which the registration is sought, were just indicative examples drawn from a number of factors which can be taken into account (Para. 20). In the context of the overall analysis, account may also be taken of i) the origin of the sign (filed for preventing a third party´s sign) and its use since its creation and ii) the commercial logic underlying the registration of that sign as a CTM (Para. 21). 

The fact that the CTM applicant knows or must know that a third party has long been using, in at least one Member State, an identical or similar sign for identical or similar goods, which could give rise to confusion with the sign for which registration is sought, is not sufficient in itself to permit the conclusion that the applicant was acting in bad faith (Para, 27). Moreover, the good faith of an applicant cannot be challenged solely because it is the proprietor of other marks and did not take the initiative to register them as CTMs (Para. 28).
The GC found that it matters little whether or not the goods marketed by the invalidity applicant under the mark BIGA are similar or identical to the goods marketed by the CTM owner, since, in any event, it has not been shown that the sole purpose of the CTM registration was to prevent the invalidity applicant from using the mark BIGA (Para. 32). Similarly, the fact that, a few weeks after successfully registering the mark BIGAB as a CTM, its owner sent a cease and desist letter to the invalidity applicant is not an indication of bad faith, since such a request falls within the scope of the rights attached to a CTM (Article 9 CTMR) (Para. 33). Finally, the fact that the Board of Appeal has relied on the reference in Article 12 CTMR to ‘honest practices in industrial or commercial matters’, for the purposes of defining ‘bad faith’ as used in Article 52(1)(b), was not considered an error in law, even though the subject-matter of the two provisions is different. The GC held that the Board did not err in law by considering the two provisions in parallel, as this was justified by the fact that they are both designed to penalize commercial dishonesty (Para. 35 & 36). 
Conclusion: In essence, the GC held that the intention of the owner of a national mark to protect it ‘on a European scale’ does not constitute a bad faith action against a similar national right designating similar goods, especially when this mark has been previously used in various EU countries and predates the national sign. It matters little in that context that the CTM applicant knew or, at least, should have known, at the time of filing the CTMA, of the use of the other sign which could give rise to confusion with the sign for which registration is sought.

results.quotation: (01/02/2012, T‑291/09, Pollo Tropical CHICKEN ON THE GRILL, EU:T:2012:39)
results.entityName: Pollo Tropical CHICKEN ON THE GRILL
results.nickName: Pollo Tropical CHICKEN ON THE GRILL
results.summary:
On 20 June 1994, the intervener in the case before the GC (the ïntervener¨, CTM owner) filed an application with the OEPM for registration of the following Spanish figurative mark, registered on 20 December 1995
 

On 21 October 1994, the predecessor of the applicant in the case before the GC (the ¨invalidity applicant¨ in the case before OHIM) sought to register with the OEPM the word mark POLLO TROPICAL and a figurative mark, identical to its earlier US mark, for which registration had been applied on 25 April 1994. The applications were refused due to the opposition lodged by the intervener on the basis of the Spanish mark.

On 9 June 2000, the invalidity applicant obtained registration in the United Kingdom of the word mark POLLO TROPICAL and the figurative mark reproduced below for the provision of food and drink under Class 42.
 
On 22 November 2002, the intervener applied for the following CTM for goods and services in Classes 25, 41 and 43.  It was registered on 21 April 2004.
 
On 22 January 2007, the invalidity applicant lodged an application for the declaration of invalidity of the CTM, on the grounds of likelihood of confusion in the UK (based on the earlier UK marks) and bad faith (based, inter alia, on the earlier US mark).

The Cancellation Division rejected the invalidity application.  The applicant failed to adduce proof of genuine use of its UK marks.  Bad faith was not an issue, as there was a continuity or ‘commercial trajectory’ between the Spanish mark and the CTM.  An email correspondence from 2006 in which the intervener fixed a price for the sale of the CTM was not considered conclusive proof of bad faith. 
 
The Board of Appeal dismissed the action of the invalidity applicant.  Bad faith had to be established at the time of the application for a CTM, i.e. in 2002; any bad faith at the time of the application for registration in Spain in 1994 could not be taken into consideration.  No evidence has been adduced showing that the intervener was aware of the applicant’s commercial activities in the US.  The application for a CTM was a normal and foreseeable step in the intervener’s services after it had launched its commercial activity in Spain.  Furthermore, the invalidity applicant did not file an opposition to the CTM application.

Before the General Court the invalidity applicant alleged infringement of Art. 52(1)(b) CTMR, on the ground that the intervener acted in bad faith when the CTM was applied for.  

SUBSTANCE: The General Court dismissed the action. 

The invalidity applicant has not provided any evidence establishing a presumption that the intervener could not be unaware of the existence of the US mark (paragraph 62).  

The well-known character of the US mark has not been established at the time of the application for registration of either the Spanish mark or the CTM.  No document indicates the market share or demonstrates intensity of use and magnitude of investments.  No third-party declaration attesting to the well-known character of the mark was submitted.

The invalidity applicant failed to prove genuine use of its UK mark, identical to the mark at issue.  It is doubtful that the intervener, the owner of a small pizzeria in a village on the Canary Islands, could, through its national mark, block the invalidity applicant’s expansion in the rest of the EU (paragraph 84).  

The invalidity applicant waited more than three years before asking for a declaration of invalidity of the intervener’s CTM.  

The invalidity applicant failed to establish, or even allege, that prior to the filing of the mark there was any direct or indirect relationship between the parties which might explain any bad faith on the part of the intervener (paragraph 85).  

The proposal for financial compensation in the amount of USD 5 million for the transfer of the CTM, whilst considerable, does not by itself establish bad faith on the part of the intervener at the time the CTM was filed.

The fact that the signs are identical does not establish bad faith on the part of the intervener, where there are no other factors.

results.summary: 1. The SERVEIS FUNERARIS INTEGRALS is a Catalan expression, the meaning of which is ‘integrated funeral services or complete funeral services.  2. It has very similar equivalents in the official languages of other EU Member States such as ‘INTEGRAL FUNERAL SERVICES in English, ‘SERVICES FUNERAIRES INTEGRAUX in French, or ‘SERVIZI FUNEBRI INTEGRALI in Italian. Therefore, its meaning is immediately and directly perceivable without any effort not only by Catalan consumers but also by Spanish, English, French and Italian consumers, all of whom will thus be considered relevant consumers. 3. The expression in question, which designates all funeral services, would serve to designate generically all printed matter including in memoriam cards in class 16, transport, in particular funeral transport, in class 39 and funeral services, funeral parlour services, burials, cremations and organisation of religious meetings in class 45 for which the contested trade mark is registered. 4. The graphic in the sign is so abstract as to not represent anything specific, and therefore, although it is not descriptive, it may also not be perceived or memorized by the relevant consumer as an indication of the commercial origin of the G&S. 5. The expression expression considered as a whole, being generic and descriptive of the services designated, is for that reason devoid of any distinctiveness. 6. Furthermore, the conclusion referred to corroborates the same conclusion reached by the judgments of the Spanish courts invoked by the cancellation applicant. according to settled Community case-law, registrations which have already taken place in the MS are unquestionably a factor which may only be taken into consideration, without being given decisive weight, for the purposes of registering a CTM, particularly when they refer to a situation which is factually identical to a significant degree, as occurs here. Indeed, as the Spanish Supreme Court correctly confirmed in its judgment, in deciding the extraordinary appeal brought against the judgment of the High Court of Justice of Catalonia, dismissing the appeal in administrative dispute proceedings brought against the refusal of Spanish trade mark SERVEIS FUNERARIS INTEGRALS (mixed), identical to the CTM contested here , it is quite correct to ‘find a lack of sufficient distinctiveness of the trade mark applied for ‘SERVEIS FUNERARIS INTEGRALS (mixed), to protect services in classes 39 and 45. 7. With regard to the remaining goods in class 16 and services in class 39 designated by the Community trade mark and for which cancellation was requested, in particular, the goods paper, cardboard and goods made from these materials, not included in other classes; publications; bookbinding material; photographs; stationery, in class 16, and transport, in particular ambulances, taxis and rental of cars, in class 39, it may be stated that the trade mark is devoid of any distinctive character, since although the expression examined is neither generic nor descriptive for these goods and services, that does not automatically make it distinctive and it will continue to be perceived as the designation of complete funeral services and not on that account as an indication of the commercial origin. 8. Furthermore, the proof submitted in order to demonstrate the use of the CTM is not dated or contains a date subsequent to the CTMA, and therefore cannot be taken into account in that respect, since it fails to demonstrate that on account of the use of the trade mark prior to said date of the CTMA it has become distinctive as claimed. It is also insufficient from the point of view of the territory claimed, since none of it demonstrates any kind of use of the trade mark beyond the Province of Barcelona. 9. The apepal is allowed and the registration of the CTM cancelled.
