keywords: Dissimilarity of the goods and services
Used in the course of trade
results.summary: 1. Despite the BoA's request, the applicant has failed to prove that it owns the claimed earlier right or is entitled to rely on it. The German trade mark is registered to an Eschbach Gartenausstattung GmbH rather than to the applicant by the name of Eschbach GmbH. 2. The managing directors statutory declaration and the lawyers affirmation that an Eschbach Gartenausstattung GmbH had never legally existed and Eschbach GmbH had used additional words like ‘Gartenausstattung or ‘Accente are not enough to provide such evidence. Nor do the submitted copies of extracts from the commercial register suggest that Eschbach GmbH is the same as Eschbach Gartenausstattung GmbH. In this context, it should also be borne in mind that the applicant has also obviously made no effort to correct the entry in the register of the German Patent and Trade Mark Office, or at least it has not said it has. Nor have any possible statements been made by the commercial court or in the commercial register that there has ever been a Eschbach Gartenausstattung GmbH. 3. The applicant for a declaration of invalidity used the company name ‘Eschbach GmbH in connection with ‘terracotta goods like plant pots and plant containers to an extent that was of more than merely local significance. Even if the goods in class 11 20 and ‘plant pots and plant containers made of terracotta are aimed at the general public, the intended uses of these goods are different. The goods of the CTM have nothing whatsoever to do with plants or flowers. Nor are the goods in question complementary or in competition with one another. 4. As there can be no likelihood of confusion owing to a lack of similarity between the goods, the application is also to be refused on the basis of Article 53(1)(c) in conjunction with Article 8(4) CTMR. 5. The appeal is allowed.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary:
I. FACTS IN BRIEF
The Institute for Personality & Ability Testing, Inc, filed on 6 October 2000, an application (CTM No 1892652) for registration of the word sign ‘16PF’, in respect of goods and services in Classes 9, 16, 35, 41 and 42. The CTM was registered on 17 March 2003. Psytech International Ltd (the appellant), filed an application for a declaration of invalidity against the CTM 16PF based on Article 52(1)(a), read in conjunction with Article 7(1)(b) to (d) and Article 52(1)(b) CTMR.
The Cancellation Division dismissed the application for a declaration of invalidity. The applicant for cancellation filed an appeal.

The Board of Appeal (R 1012/2007-2) dismissed the appeal. In essence, the BoA after stating that 16PF was the abbreviation of ‘16 personality factors’ and designated a specific behavioural test developed by Dr Cattell, found that none of the provisions of Article 7(1) CTMR invoked was applicable since the sign in question was sufficiently distinctive, was not descriptive and had not become customary. It also found that no act of bad faith had been proved.

The applicant for cancellation filed an appeal before the GC claiming infringement of Article 7(1)(c), (d) and (b) CTMR and Article 52(1)(b) CTMR.

II. FINDINGS OF THE COURT  	

1.	The first plea: infringement of Article 7(1)(c) CTMR

The GC recalled the applicable case-law to the assessment of descriptive signs (paras.22-26). The GC confirmed that the relevant public is both the general public and professionals in the field of personality assessment and personality testing (para.29).

The GC decided that the applicant for cancellation did not put forward any grounds to invalidate the Board’s assessment that the mark is not descriptive of the G&S or that it designates one of their characteristics (para.32-33).

The GC considered proven that as regards the specialised public, the link between the sign and the characteristics of the G&S is not sufficiently ‘clear and direct’. The fact that some of the G&S at issue relate to a system of personality analysis based on 16 factors is not sufficient to regard the sign 16PF as descriptive of those G&S. The average professional in the human resources sector would not understand the meaning of the mark without a complex process of interpretation involving time of reflection and a significant amount of imagination. The GC pointed out that, even if a section of the relevant public could perceive, without further thought, the mark as referring to Dr Cattell’s 16 factor personality test that would suggest that that mark performs the essential function of a trade mark which is to allow identification of commercial origin (paras.39-42). In relation to the general public, the sign will be perceived as a sign which is devoid of any meaning by all those who do not have a very good knowledge of behavioural studies and, therefore, as non-descriptive (para.43). The plea was dismissed.

2. Infringment of Article 7(1)(d) CTMR
The GC underlined that the applicant for cancellation has not been able to provide sufficient evidence to show that the sign 16PF has actually become customary. The evidence provided shows that the mark 16PF is widely used to designate the specific origin of the G&S in question (paras.57-58).
The applicant for cancellation claimed that 16PF is the generic name of a test and, consequently, of all the printed matter in Class 16. However, he did not claim that 16PF was the customary designation for the other G&S in Classes 9, 35, 41 and 42. The GC concluded that the application of Article 7(1)(d) CTMR must therefore be excluded in respect of those classes of G&S (paras.59-60).
In relation with the goods in Class 16 the GC could state that the use of the sign 16PF in the trade, except by the CTM owner, was limited to one single trader in the EU and that use came to an end following the CTM owner’s reaction. Consequently, even if that use had begun in 1999, the GC considered difficult that when the CTMA was filed, one year later, the sign 16PF had become a generic term to designate questionnaires other than those of the CTM owner (paras.61-65). The plea was dismissed.
3. Infringment of Article 7(1)(b) CTMR
In relation to this ground, the GC pointed out that that the applicant for cancellation relies on the descriptive character of the mark to draw conclusions as regards its alleged absence of distinctive character. The GC held, in the context of the analysis of the first plea that the BoA was right in finding that the mark was not descriptive. The plea was dismissed (paras.78-80). 

4. Infringment of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR
The GC rejected the applicant’s arguments in relation to the registration of the CTM in bad faith.  
The GC stated that the fact that the applicant for the trade mark knows or must know that a third party has for a long time been using, in at least one MS, an identical or similar sign for an identical or similar product capable of being confused with the sign for which registr

1.	The application for a declaration of invalidity is inadmissible as it is an abuse of law. Admittedly, the objection of res judicata is not an obstacle to the admissibility of the application for a declaration of invalidity. The decision of the Second Board of Appeal of 16 December 2003 in Case R 375/2001-2 is not binding upon the presiding Board, either with regard to the Member States in respect of which a ground for refusal was to be affirmed, or with regard to the appreciation of the (identical) documents on distinctiveness in consequence of use, or as regards the question of whether this was actually necessary). It was not given in the same type of proceedings and the applicant was not party to those proceedings. The contested decision implicitly made that assumption when it based itself on, grounds for refusal (grounds for invalidity) in Member States other than those examined by the Second Board of Appeal.2.	The abuse of law as a general impediment to actions and proceedings is to be recognised as part of Community law. The present case, however, does not involve a general need for legal protection of the individual concern through a measure, but rather the preclusion of the misuse of applications and actions, therefore court and judicial proceedings by means of which aims other than those covered by the law are being pursued. There was therefore no need even for the investigation and citation of procedural law of the Member States (Article 83 CTMR). The trade mark proprietors citation of the CDs decision of 11 May 2001 in Case 491 C, ‘EXAKTA, and the principles of national law discussed therein, such as ‘clean hands, was not relevant, since no decision was made in those proceedings regarding inadmissibility. Upon appeal by the trade mark proprietor, the contested decision is to be annulled and the application for a declaration of invalidity is to be refused.3.	The Board annuls the contested decision and refuses the application for a declaration of invalidity.

keywords: Contrary to public policy or principles of morality
results.summary: 1. The invalidity applicant has failed to demonstrate to what extent the CTM proprietor acted in bade faith at the time it applied for the CTM. 2. Thus for this reason alone, the application for invalidity pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR is to be refused. 3. Nor is the Board able to discern any action in bad faith on the part of the proprietor just because of having filed several notices of opposition against CTM applications because it feared confusion with its own trade mark. 4. Nor can it be said that there has been a wave of warnings by the proprietor. 5. The trade mark was published on 10 December 2001 and, following the conclusion of several opposition proceedings, was entered in the register on 16 November 2007. 6. It should further be pointed out that it is not a condition for the registration of a trade mark that the trade mark applicants field of business coincides with the list of goods and services. 7. As to the applicants claim under Article 7(1)(f), the Board notes that the wording of Article 7(1)(f)  is very broadly formulated and allows considerable room for interpretation. 8. For a judicious application of this provision, the right of companies freely to use words and images in the signs which they wish to have registered as trade marks must be weighed up against the right of the general public not to be confronted with disturbing, offensive, insulting or even threatening trade marks. 9. The applicant for a declaration of invalidity has failed to state in what way the trade mark could be contrary to public policy and accepted principles of morality. 10. The sign protected thereby is neither offensive nor insulting and nor does the list of goods and services cover goods or services which could be subject to a statutory interdiction of commerce. 11. In the Boards view, the registered CTM is a purely fanciful word to which no meaning can be ascribed. 12. It may well be the case that the sign evokes associations with the English word ‘yellow; this association may indeed even have been desired by the trade mark applicant. 13. However, possible association is not sufficient in order to take away from the Community trade mark any distinctive character. 14. Precisely the absence of the final letter contributes to the consumer engaging intellectually with the sign and to it therefore also being remembered. 15. Even if consumers were to recognize the English term ‘yellow in the CTM, the connection with the relevant G&S is missing. 16. As the Board is of the opinion that the Community trade mark is distinctive, the application based on Article 52(1)(a) in conjunction with Article 7(1)(c) CTMR is to be refused. 17. The appeal is dismissed.

keywords: Dissimilarity of the goods and services
results.summary: 1. The contested decision contains some errors as regards the relevant territories of the international registrations on which the application for a declaration of invalidity is based. 2. It further states that the request for POU of the earlier CTM No 1 476 456 is unfounded, even though the CTM proprietor had, rightly, not requested it. 3. However, those errors have no bearing on the outcome of the application for a declaration of invalidity. 4. The cancellation applicant does not dispute the findings of the CD as regards POU of the earlier Italian marks No 333 765/918 023, 409 541, 641 466, and international registrations 675 197, 471 369 and 733 009. 5. The Board concurs with the CD as to the dissimilarity of the goods in the case of the Italian trade marks No 333 765/918 023, 409 541 and 641 466. 6. With regard to the comparison with the earlier CTM No 1 476 456, the cancellation applicant fails to explain why the comparison made in the contested decision is incorrect. 7. Its argument based on the fact that the contested trade mark constitutes a reproduction of the earlier trade mark with the simple addition of the generic elements ‘1, 2, 3 and ‘STRONG is not convincing. 8. Indeed, the earlier CTM is composed of word elements which are purely descriptive of the purpose of the goods. 9. The earlier mark owes its distinctive character solely to its figurative elements, which are not reproduced in the contested CTM. 10. Furthermore, the elements ‘FRESH and ‘CLEAN are not reproduced in the same order, and the addition of the figures ‘1, 2, 3 and the term ‘STRONG gives the trade mark a completely different overall visual appearance and rhythm. 11. The Board concludes that the similarities between the marks are insufficient to give rise to LOC when applied to the contested goods in Classes 3 and 5. 12. The cancellation applicant has not furnished sufficient proof of the reputation of its trade marks. 13. According to the extracts from the 2001 survey conducted in Italy, although the level of ‘prompted recognition stands at 86 %, the rate of ‘spontaneous recognition is only 56 %. 14. Moreover, no indication is given of the questions put to the people surveyed, making it impossible to determine whether the questions were really open and unassisted. 15. The survey further fails to state in relation to which goods the trade mark is known. 16. The mere existence of the earlier trade mark on the market, even if its reputation had been demonstrated, does not constitute bad faith within the meaning of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR since the contested trade mark is incapable of being confused with the earlier marks. 17. Moreover, no arguments have been advanced demonstrating fraudulent intent on the part of the trade mark proprietor. 18. The appeal is dismissed.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
keywords: Sign used in the course of trade
results.summary: 1. The evidence produced does not demonstrate that the respondent has used the sign in the course of trade before the date in which the contested CTM was applied for. 2. Indeed, there are only two invoices from 1998 in which the respondent appears as the seller of the ‘COLOURBLIND product and this is not sufficient to prove that the respondent used the trade mark at issue in the course of trade. 3. The rest of the invoices submitted show that Future Factory Limited was the provider of the products with the trade mark ‘COLOURBLIND but not the respondent. 4. The respondent states that due to the group structure of the companies there was an implied licence from the respondent to Future Factory Ltd. to allow it to make use of the intellectual property rights in the mark at issue; however such statement without any further evidence to support it is not sufficient to demonstrate the existence of the indicated licence. 5. The combined wording of Article 53 (1) (c) CTMR and Article 8 (4) CTMR clearly states that only the proprietor of the (alleged) unregistered right can apply for declaration of invalidity and that only the natural or legal person who used the mark can acquire the rights to which Article 8 (4) CTMR refers. 6. The conclusion that there was bad faith from the side of the appellant based on a ‘common understanding or on the belief that an entire commercial relationship had given the respondent good reason to rely on the notion that the trade mark was of its property is based on vague considerations. 7. Expressions such as ‘implied licence, ‘informal business relationship cannot result in the conclusion of bad faith. 8. The respondent has not provided any documents evidencing that Mr. Cox, who created the toolbox ‘COLOURBLIND and coined the term ‘COLOURBLIND for the products, transferred exclusive rights on the trade mark to it. 9. Bad faith cannot be based on abstract concepts such as ‘common understanding or indicating that ‘a behaviour during an entire commercial relationship had given the respondent good reason to rely on the notion that the trade mark ‘COLOURBLIND was of its property. 10. Bad faith must be demonstrated without subjective interpretations; it cannot be used as a tool to compensate the absence of agreements and/or stipulations that should have been made in due time but were not made. 11. The contested decision is annulled and both grounds for cancellation, under Article 52 (1) (c) in conjunction with Article 8 (4) CTMR as well as under Article 52 (1) (b) CTMR (bad faith), are dismissed.

keywords: Used in the course of trade
results.summary: 1. The appellant claims that the respondent was acting in bad faith because it has registered the mark and is asking it for the payment of royalties and prohibits the use of the trade mark Alphatec in breach of an agreement signed back in 1993 (in Japanese) between the AK founders GNG and YTO, which clearly establishes that GNG is entitled to use the trade mark Alphatec but without the possibility of claiming royalties or licenses to any company belonging to the business group. 2. Since the appellant has refused to pay the royalties, it has been required by the respondents lawyers to stop using the mark. 3. As to the agreement in question, the Board cannot be sure of its real content since there are three different translations into English. 4. However, it appears to be clear that nothing in that agreement prohibits GNG to ask for the payment of royalties for the use of the mark. 5. Such possibility is also supported, inter alia, by the invoices proposed for Alphatec Hungary and Hispania covering the use of the Alphatec brand dated 11 June 2002. 6. The appellant tries to give little relevance to its relation with Alphatec Engineering Pte. Ltd. by emphasizing its business relations with other companies of the Alphatec group. 6. However, it cannot be denied that the appellant sold products which were imported from Alphatec Engineering Pte. Ltd. in Singapore, precisely because the Spanish company did not have the know-how to manufacture those products itself. 7. The appellant has not only not been able to demonstrate the existence of bad faith on the side of the respondent, but has also not played with open cards during these proceedings. 8. The Board cannot understand why the appellant did not provide clear, and all the available, information in the first instance with its request for cancellation. 9. The new evidence submitted by the parties at appeal state shall not be taken into account. 10. The Board, in exercising its discretion under Article 76 CTMR comes to the conclusion that all this evidence should have been presented in support of the initial cancellation request. 11. A party claiming bad faith should not withhold material evidence, especially when the said evidence seems to be important for deciding on the facts. 12. It should furthermore, not hide information, remain silent in relation to arguments of the other party, provide manipulated data and not give convincing explanations to justify such behaviour. 13. On the basis of the available evidence, it cannot be concluded that the CTM proprietor acted in bad faith when filing the CTM. 14. The case should not be decided against the CTM proprietor as the good faith presumption prevails, unless there are clear grounds to decide otherwise. 15. The appeal is dismissed.

1.	The burden of proof is on the cancellation applicant; in the absence of evidence that the CTM proprietor has acted in bad faith when filing the CTM, the Office may not declare the invalidity of the registration of the CTM. It is true that there are several differences between the registered signs  (CTM-1a and 1b) and  (CTM-2). On the other hand these alterations (including a different font, a somewhat different ‘pelican and a different amount of ‘chicks) could be considered as insignificant in the sense that the CTM-2 differs in elements that do not alter the distinctive character of CTM-1a. Nonetheless, although the differences between the two marks are perhaps insignificant from a trade mark point of view, this does not automatically mean that the second application is a (repetitive) application made in bad faith. A trade mark proprietor might very well register an ‘up-dated version of its previous registered trade mark which at its turn is also an ‘up-dated version of a prior registered trade mark, not because his sole objective is to prevent a third party from entering the market but, for example, and as also alleged by the CTM proprietor, to meet evolving market requirements. This is totally normal business practice and is especially common concerning figurative logos which have a long lifespan. In the present case, the extract of the annual report 2003 of the Pelikan Holding AG seems to confirm that the application of CTM-2 serves to file - on the 125th ‘birthday of the CTM proprietor and which coincides as to its time with the lapse of the so called grace period of CTM-1a and CTM-1b - a modernized version of CTM-1a (and previous logos, which go all the way back to 1878).  Moreover, the extracts from the CTM proprietors Brand and Design Manual of January 2003 which concern detailed information on how the new corporate logo has to be used ‘…in ALL means of Pelikan information are a further indication that the CTM proprietor did not file CTM-2 with the sole objective of it being to prevent a third party from entering the market.  It might very well be that a CTM proprietor modernizes his trade mark on a regular basis and with the best intentions for certain goods and services, but not for all goods and services. Taking into account all the facts, evidence and arguments submitted by both parties, the Board considers that the cancellation applicant did not prove that the CTM proprietor was acting in bad faith when it filed the application for the trade mark.2.	The appeal is dismissed.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary: 1. In its statements of grounds, the cancellation applicant explicitly states that the contested decision is not appealed as far as it was based on Article 53(1)(c) in conjunction with Article 8(4) CTMR since its requirements are literally not met. Therefore, the only issue still to be decided is whether the CTM proprietor acted in bad faith when it filed the contested application. 2. In the case at present, there is no need for the suspension of the proceedings as requested by the cancellation applicant, since the facts and the evidence do not show that the CTM proprietor acted in bad faith when applying for the contested trade mark. 3. Furthermore, it must be remembered that although the Dutch Court could come to the conclusion that the Benelux trade mark ‘BIGAB filed by the CTM proprietor was filed in bad faith, this would not necessarily imply that there was also bad faith when applying for the contested CTM, taking also into account that the CTM system is an autonomous system with its own set of rules and objectives peculiar to it which applies independently of any other system. 4. The fact that the company from which the CTM proprietor acquired its business is called ‘Blidsberg Investment Group BIG AB from which the contested trade mark takes the initial letters and the fact that this company created the trade mark ‘BIGAB in 1991, years before the cancellation applicant started to use the sign ‘BIGA, in 1996, proves that the CTM proprietor did not act in bad faith when applying for the contested CTM. In this context, as the contested decision correctly pointed out, the creation and use of the sign ‘BIGAB by its predecessors in law must be attributed also to the CTM proprietor as when a business is transferred, all unregistered signs used in the business are embraced by the transfer, unless they have been explicitly excluded from the transfer, which apparently was not the case. 5. Even if the the CTM proprietor should have known that the cancellation applicant was using the sign ‘BIGA for ‘vertical feed mixers and independently of whether or not the protection of an unregistered trade mark in the Benelux enjoys a ‘sufficient degree of legal protection within the meaning of the ‘Lindt Goldhase judgement, it has been shown that the CTM proprietor had a prior right, which predates the cancellation applicants rights to the sign ‘BIGA. For this reason it cannot be held that the CTM proprietor acted in bad faith, when it filed the contested CTM. 6. The appeal is dismissed.

1. It is not possible to object, from any legal or factual point of view, to the fact that the proprietor sought to register the designation ‘Solingen also as a Community collective mark in 2002. As a chamber of industry and trade it thus pursued the objective, laid down in its statutes and legally approved, of protecting the designation ‘Solingen against use in relation to cutting products which do not originate from Solingen. 2. Given these facts it is not possible to justify, from any legal or factual point of view, how the applicant could have acquired, as a consequence of the use of the designation ‘Solingold in relation to goods which it does not claim to have manufactured in Solingen, a property right, worthy of protection, precisely to the designation  ‘Solingen. In this respect, it is irrelevant whether the designation ‘Solingold is similar to ‘Solingen or whether the proprietor was, or must have been, aware of the use claimed by the applicant. Under no circumstances could the applicant have acquired individual legal protection for the designation ‘Solingen which must be regarded as a property right worthy of protection. 3. The application for a declaration of invalidity is intended solely to prevent the proprietor from exercising its rights in other proceedings.

1. The applicants argument that the CTM is void because of lack of distinctive capacity is unfounded since, firstly, the distinctive character of a figurative trade mark is measured, not in terms of the quantity already registered on the same subject, but how far the sign is capable of being perceived by the public in question as a trade mark. 2. In the Boards view, the design of a stylised flower, such as that registered by the proprietor, is perfectly able to function as a company trade mark. 3. It does not have any conceptual link with the goods (which are not daisies but clothes and leather goods) and may be perceived by consumers as the ‘symbol of a specific company. 4. The fact that a sign has a decorative function with regard to goods does not automatically rule out the presence of a distinctive function. 5. The aim of the ground of invalidity referred to in Article 7(1)(e) CTMR is to avoid the acquisition of exclusivity over signs which are identified with the formal aspect of the goods for which registration is requested. 6. This is not the case here. 7. The proprietors trade mark does represent a ‘product (in fact, a plant), but registration is not requested for that product. 8. Therefore, there is no formal identity between the sign and the product. 9. As regards the invalidity applicants claim under Article 53(2)(c), it should be pointed out that this ground of invalidity is relative. 10. Therefore, only holders of earlier rights - or other parties, if allowed by the law governing the said rights - are entitled to invoke it (Article 56(1)(c) CTMR). 11. The right which is to be relied upon here is copyright. 12. Therefore, the party entitled to act is the holder of the copyright in the flower design or another party authorised by the law governing copyright. 13. The invalidity applicant does not own the right it seeks to rely upon since it acknowledges that ownership of the copyright in the design ‘belongs to third parties (in fact to one third party: Corel Corporation, the graphic design company). 14. The applicants claim under Article 53(1)(c) is erroneous because copyright cannot be invoked for the purposes of Article 8(4) since it is not a sign. 15. The applicants claim under Articles 53(1)(a) is also rejected since copyright cannot be relied upon in proceedings for invalidity based on Article 53(1) CTMR. 16. The appeal is rejected.

1. The cancellation applicant based its application on a Polish ‘company name, namely its own corporate name, whose legally recognised abbreviation is, according to the Companies Register, ‘FAGUMIT Sp. Zo.o. (in English: FAGUMIT plc). The company was established, according to the evidence, in 1993. Article 165(4)(b) CTMR determines that a CTM may not be declared invalid if the invoked national right has been acquired in a new Member State prior to the date of accession. This is the case of the cancellation applicants company name, to which rights were acquired before Polands accession (in 2004). As a result, the CTM may not be declared invalid on the basis of these rights.2. The cancellation applicant also claimed use of an unregistered sign in Germany prior to the CTM filing date (18 February 2003). The sign is a company name. The cancellation applicant asserts, in particular, that it exported rubber products under its company name to a German dealer, Adamex, prior to 18 February 2003. The cancellation applicant proved that assertion with the copy of a distributorship agreement dated 1 June 1997, and the CTM proprietor, who was at the time the CEO of Adamex, confirmed, on the one hand, the existence of the agreement and, on the other hand, the use of the FAGUMIT name in Germany. The Board considers that the statements, moreover made by a non-interested party, are a reliable indication that use of the unregistered sign has been made ‘in the course of trade, because selling a product ‘labelled with the sign by means of a distribution agreement amounts to use of the mark in trade. Use was, in addition of ‘more than mere local significance because the goods were exported to ‘Germany, and to several other Member States. Use, therefore, was of national and even international significance. The Agreement is an ordinary distribution agreement which was meant to function as follows according to the intention of the parties: (i) the cancellation applicant produces the goods in its Polish factory and brands them with the FAGUMIT name; (ii) it sells them (already branded) to Adamex, in Germany; (iii) Adamex resells the branded goods in Germany and elsewhere. The condition requiring use of the sign in trade is therefore fulfilled. The invalidity action is therefore successful under Article 53(1)(c)CTMR.3.	There is a substantial difference between the right to use the mark and the ownership of the mark. Thus, there is no clear evidence that the cancellation applicant gave consent to registration of the mark and, in any event, no evidence at all that the consent was for the registration of the FAGUMIT mark in the agents name. The ground of invalidity is accordingly well founded.4.	The cancellation applicant indicates that business signs are protected by Article 5 of the German Trade Mark Law. This is correct. Business signs (Geschaeftliche Bezeichnungen) are protected and these signs include, according to Article 5(2), names under which an undertaking carries out business. The cancellation applicant carries out business under its FAGUMIT corporate name and that name is used on the products exported to Germany and resold there by Adamex. It follows that it is entitled to protection under German law.  The invalidity action is therefore successful under Article 53(1)(c)CTMR.5.	The evidence in the file demonstrates that Ms Adamowski (agent) knew, on 18 February 2003, that: (i) the mark belonged to the cancellation applicant; (ii) there was an on-going commercial agreement; (iii) that the cancellation applicant was exporting to Germany products bearing the mark; (iv) a fax dated 10 April 1998 existed. She should have realised that the fax, written by a foreign person, was oddly formulated and could produce the result of the cancellation applicant losing, unintentionally and without any reasonable explanation, all its rights. If she had been acting in good faith, as she claims, she would have sought clarification from Mr Gorecki, prior to filing the CTM application, as to whether he really meant to surrender his mark. The Board underlines, in this regard, that to do so was also required under Clause 2 of the Commercial Agreement. By applying to register the word element of the cancellation applicants mark in her name in the above circumstances, the CTM proprietor manifestly displayed bad faith.6.	The Board annuls the contested decision and declares the invalidity of CTM No 3 093 226.

1. The cancellation applicant based its application on a Polish ‘company name, namely its own corporate name, whose legally recognised abbreviation is, according to the Companies Register, ‘FAGUMIT Sp. Zo.o. (in English: FAGUMIT plc). The company was established, according to the evidence, in 1993. Article 165(4)(b) CTMR determines that a CTM may not be declared invalid if the invoked national right has been acquired in a new Member State prior to the date of accession. This is the case of the cancellation applicants company name, to which rights were acquired before Polands accession (in 2004). As a result, the CTM may not be declared invalid on the basis of these rights.2. The cancellation applicant also claimed use of an unregistered sign in Germany prior to the CTM filing date (18 February 2003). The sign is a company name. The cancellation applicant asserts, in particular, that it exported rubber products under its company name to a German dealer, Adamex, prior to 18 February 2003. The cancellation applicant proved that assertion with the copy of a distributorship agreement dated 1 June 1997, and the CTM proprietor, who was at the time the CEO of Adamex, confirmed, on the one hand, the existence of the agreement and, on the other hand, the use of the FAGUMIT name in Germany. The Board considers that the statements, moreover made by a non-interested party, are a reliable indication that use of the unregistered sign has been made ‘in the course of trade, because selling a product ‘labelled with the sign by means of a distribution agreement amounts to use of the mark in trade. Use was, in addition of ‘more than mere local significance because the goods were exported to ‘Germany, and to several other Member States. Use, therefore, was of national and even international significance. The Agreement is an ordinary distribution agreement which was meant to function as follows according to the intention of the parties: (i) the cancellation applicant produces the goods in its Polish factory and brands them with the FAGUMIT name; (ii) it sells them (already branded) to Adamex, in Germany; (iii) Adamex resells the branded goods in Germany and elsewhere. The condition requiring use of the sign in trade is therefore fulfilled. The invalidity action is therefore successful under Article 53(1)(c)CTMR.3.	There is a substantial difference between the right to use the mark and the ownership of the mark. Thus, there is no clear evidence that the cancellation applicant gave consent to registration of the mark and, in any event, no evidence at all that the consent was for the registration of the FAGUMIT mark in the agents name. The ground of invalidity is accordingly well founded.4.	The cancellation applicant indicates that business signs are protected by Article 5 of the German Trade Mark Law. This is correct. Business signs (Geschaeftliche Bezeichnungen) are protected and these signs include, according to Article 5(2), names under which an undertaking carries out business. The cancellation applicant carries out business under its FAGUMIT corporate name and that name is used on the products exported to Germany and resold there by Adamex. It follows that it is entitled to protection under German law.  The invalidity action is therefore successful under Article 53(1)(c)CTMR.5.	The evidence in the file demonstrates that Ms Adamowski (agent) knew, on 18 February 2003, that: (i) the mark belonged to the cancellation applicant; (ii) there was an on-going commercial agreement; (iii) that the cancellation applicant was exporting to Germany products bearing the mark; (iv) a fax dated 10 April 1998 existed. She should have realised that the fax, written by a foreign person, was oddly formulated and could produce the result of the cancellation applicant losing, unintentionally and without any reasonable explanation, all its rights. If she had been acting in good faith, as she claims, she would have sought clarification from Mr Gorecki, prior to filing the CTM application, as to whether he really meant to surrender his mark. The Board underlines, in this regard, that to do so was also required under Clause 2 of the Commercial Agreement. By applying to register the word element of the cancellation applicants mark in her name in the above circumstances, the CTM proprietor manifestly displayed bad faith.6.	The Board annuls the contested decision and declares the invalidity of CTM No 3 093 226.

keywords: Sign used in the course of trade
results.summary: 1. The cancellation applicant used the term ‘CLOCHE FRAICHEUR only as a mention on a label, not as a trade mark. 2. The CTM proprietor has even pointed out that the Community registration does not prevent the use of terms on packaging, when they are not used as a trade mark, and that it has never invoked or attempted to invoke its ‘CLOCHE FRAICHEUR trade mark in opposition to the cancellation applicants use of a mention consisting of said term on packaging. 3. Therefore, it has not been demonstrated that the CTM proprietor intended, when filing its CTM, to prevent the cancellation applicant from continuing to use the ‘CLOCHE FRAICHEUR sign as a term on packaging. 4. Moreover, the CTM proprietor has adduced documents showing that, after registration of the ‘CLOCHE FRAICHEUR expression, it affixed said term to one of its products, namely ‘Chaumes cheese. 5. The use of the sign in business is likewise proof of the trade mark owners interest in genuinely using the sign and that the reason for filing the registration of the CTM was not to prevent the cancellation applicant from continuing to use the expression ‘CLOCHE FRAICHEUR. 6. Lastly, in so far as the expression ‘CLOCHE FRAICHEUR has been known since 1999 as a mention on packaging, it is unlikely that the CTM proprietor would have waited six years before filing an application with the sole, principal aim of harming the cancellation applicants interests. 7. Consequently, the cancellation applicant has not demonstrated the existence of bad faith on the part of the CTM proprietor.8. The appeal is dismissed.

results.quotation: (18/08/2010, R 0559/2009‑4, SEBA TRADITION ESTABLIHED 1932 20 FILTER CIGARETTES (fig.))
keywords: Identity of the goods and services
Similarity of the goods and services
results.summary: 1.	The identical word ‘TRADITION is the dominant element in the overall visual impression and the signs have a high degree of similarity despite the differences in the remaining elements and in the colours of the image. The signs are also conceptually similar, because the consumers in question will recognise the same component, ‘TRADITION,in both of the trade marks owing to its emphasised position. There is therefore a likelihood of confusion for the German public taking account of the identity or similarity between the goods in Class 34 of the two trade marks, the average distinctive character, the aural identity of the signs and the visual and conceptual similarity.2. The respondent exclusively invoked the relative ground for refusal of likelihood of confusion as a ground for invalidity in its application for a declaration of invalidity of 23 November 2007. In respect of the relative ground for invalidity of an agent trade mark in conjunction with Article 8(3) CTMR), which was only first put forth by the letter of 26 May 2008, and the absolute ground for invalidity of an application made in bad faith, the application is therefore inadmissible.3.	The appeal is dismissed.

keywords: Dissimilarity of the goods and services
results.summary: 1.	The contested goods ‘trunks; umbrellas in Class 18 are dissimilar to the goods covered by the earlier marks in Class 25, namely ‘clothing, footwear and headgear, including hats, shirts, T-shirts, jackets and light jackets, gloves, trousers and shorts, socks, headbands, wristbands, neckerchiefs and underwear including for sports purposes. Thus, ‘trunks can be used for storage or for travelling and that manufacturers of travelling bags and valises may also manufacture trunks.  However, the vast majority of manufacturers of clothing, footwear or headgear do not normally produce and sell luggage. Furthermore, ‘umbrellas are normally not considered fashion accessories.  In most cases umbrellas are sold in the same outlets as leather items like bags and purses, as the cancellation applicant itself alleges.  Thus, ‘umbrellas are not normally sold in the same outlets as clothing, footwear and headgear. Therefore, notwithstanding the similarity of the signs, there is no likelihood of confusion as regards the contested goods ‘trunks; umbrellas in Class 18.2.	Even if in the present case the marks are sufficiently close to justify the application of Article 8(3) CTMR (if it were admitted that the CTM proprietor is an agent of the cancellation applicant within the meaning of Article 8(3) CTMR), to the extent that the sign applied for identically reproduces two of the earlier marks in a combined form, the same cannot be said for the contested goods ‘leather and imitations of leather; trunks; umbrellas in Class 18 when compared to the goods covered in Class 25 by the earlier marks. Consequently, the Cancellation Division rightly rejected the request for a declaration of invalidity based on Article 8(3) CTMR.3.	The appeal is dismissed.

results.summary: 1. The earlier mark is a highly complex trade mark with the imposing and dominant dandelion seed head with some seeds dispersed or blown off words against a blue background. 2. The CTM applied for contains no figurative elements nor uses any colours. 3. However, it reproduces the descriptive wording ‘es fácil dejar de fumar ¡… si sabes cómo ! in the earlier mark No 2 555 244 and ‘es fácil ¡… si sabes cómo ! in the earlier mark No 2 555 245. 4. The structure of the CTM applied for, thus, creates an entirely different overall visual impression. 5. Moreover, the publics attention will be drawn to the words ‘ALLEN CARRS EASYWAY at the beginning of the CTM applied for, none of which belong to the Spanish language. 6. Accordingly, the visual differences between the signs are significantly greater than the similarity based on the wording ‘es fácil dejar de fumar ¡… si sabes cómo! or ‘es fácil ¡… si sabes cómo!. 7. The CTM applied for will be pronounced by the most distinctive wording ‘ALLEN CARRS EASYWAY. 8. However the earlier marks are pronounced, the pronunciation of the mark applied for as ‘ALLEN CARRS EASYWAY differentiates the marks aurally. 9. Conceptually, the similarity between the marks arises solely on account of terms that lack distinctive character. 10. The conceptual similarity is therefore not decisive. In their overall impression, the marks are not similar. 11. When applied to the contested G&S in Classes 9, 16, 41, 44 and 45, there is no LOC. 12. The opposition based on Article 8(5) CTMR is unfounded since no evidence of reputation has been submitted. 13. The opponents allegation that the applicant has used the option of filing a CTM  application in an effort to avoid a Spanish application and on the grounds that the ‘applicant no longer knows how to attempt to conceal its objective attempt at misappropriation effectively amounts to an allegation of bad faith. 14. However, bad faith in filing the CTM application is an argument that cannot be assessed by the Board within the scope of opposition proceedings because bad faith as such does not constitute a ground for opposition but only an absolute ground for declaration of invalidity under Article 52(1) (b) CTMR. 15. The appeal is dismissed.

1.	The fact that the applicant knew of TMMRs intention to use the trade mark ‘SLIME is not sufficient in itself to assume bad faith. If the use of a sign is merely planned, as in this case, the only arguments which can apply are those which apply in cases of actual prior use. In this case, the fact that the applicant knows or must know that a third party has long been using, in at least one Member State, an identical or similar sign for an identical or similar product capable of being confused with the sign for which registration is sought is not sufficient, in itself, to permit the conclusion that the applicant was acting in bad faith. Furthermore, the statement by the applicant for a declaration of invalidity about a so-called ‘repeat registration provides no reason to assume bad faith. Thus, bad faith on the part of the applicant in filing the CTM application cannot be established.2.	The appeal is dismissed.

results.summary: 1.	The CTM proprietor does not dispute the fact that he was a member of the WUKO / EKU Commission from 1977 to 1988. He is a member of the amateur association with the name of the World Union of Karate-do Organization, an organization different from that of the cancellation applicant, the purposes of which are to promote and regulate karate at an educational, ethical and sports level. The CTM proprietor is therefore a member of an association in the karate field which uses the same acronym and name as the ones which the cancellation applicant has used and which it continues to be known by. The CTM proprietor has advanced no proof that ‘WUKO is used by other organizations. In addition, both the CTM and the cancellation applicants sign are circular, with the wording ‘WUKO in a central position and around which appears a five pointed flower like figurative element. There is a strong resemblance between the conflicting signs. Consequently, it results from the forgoing that the cancellation applicant has met the onus of showing that in filing the application for the CTM, without the cancellation applicants consent, the CTM proprietor dishonestly sought to lay its hands on its ‘WUKO sign in a manner that any reasonably commercially minded person would consider falls short of standards of acceptable commercial behaviour. Thus, the CTM proprietor acted in bad faith in filing the CTM application. Accordingly, the CTM is declared invalid.2.	The appeal is dismissed.

results.summary: 1. The evidence of reputation should have been evaluated taking account of the particular nature of the case in hand: the fact that the applicant does not carry on ‘commercial activity and that its public is not the general public. 2. Salini Costruttori S.p.A. carries out large-scale engineering and infrastructure projects of national significance. This is certainly an entrepreneurial activity but it is not correct to define it as ‘commercial. 3. In the view of the BoA, the documentation submitted is sufficient to prove that ‘SALINI has been used by the applicant as a trade mark in the course of its entrepreneurial activities in Italy and, in addition, is generally well known. The construction of large-scale projects such as motorways, tunnels for the High Speed Trains, Court buildings are activities which are of great interest, perhaps not among the general population, but certainly with the specific public which is relevant for these purposes, the large-scale public or private clients. 4. The company brochure was certainly not able to demonstrate ‘the importance of the company in Italy but only, as has been seen, (i) how the trade mark ‘SALINI is used and (ii) what activity is carried on by the applicant. On the other hand, the articles of the influential economic daily paper give useful indications of the financial weight of Salini Costruttori S.p.A. and, indirectly, the reputation of its trade mark. 5. Since the CD had stated that the documentation was patently insufficient, the applicant had no alternative but to provide additional material on appeal: company brochures, files on completed projects, advertisements in specialist publications, contracts, photographs of exhibition stands, invoices issued to clients, etc. This documentation is admissible and confirms what was previously known: that the applicant carried out major engineering projects in Italy prior to the application for registration of the contested trade mark, always making use of the name 'SALINI' with a distinctive function. 6. It is not necessary to to spend many words on the similarity of the trade marks, as this is evident since the dominant component is identical and the only difference is the presence of commonplace words. The surname ‘SALINI is also present in the earlier trade mark. Regardless of whether ‘COSTRUTTORI is an integral part of the earlier trade mark or not, the presence of the name ‘SALINI is sufficient to create an impression of very strong similarity. In point of fact, the term ‘COSTRUTTORI only serves to indicate the sector of activity and does not have even a remotely identified value. 7. The likelihood of confusion is almost guaranteed if the two trade marks are used in identical or related activities, in other words that which the proprietor has described as ‘Building construction; repair and installation services. In fact, in relation to these activities, the earlier trade mark has already become established. 8. On the other hand, no likelihood of confusion by the reference public can be made out if the trade mark ‘GRUPPO SALINI is used for services or activities in class 36. The earlier trade mark has never been used, according to the evidence obtained, for insurance services, monetary affairs, financial affairs, or real-estate affairs. The documentation submitted shows that the applicant has been involved solely in the design and performance of large-scale projects of the kind previously alluded to. 9. There also exists proof of bad faith from the fact that, at the time of application for registration of the CTM, the proprietor held a significant interest in the share capital of the applicant, the directors of the proprietor were members of the Board of Directors of the applicant and the proprietor could not fail to know that the trade mark ‘SALINI belonged to a ‘third party, namely the applicant for cancellation. 10. For these reasons, the application for cancellation of the CTM must be upheld and the appeal is allowed.

results.summary: 1. The word ‘exclusively, in Article 7(1)(e)(ii) CTMR must be read in the light of ‘essential characteristics which perform a technical function. 2. It is sufficient that the essential characteristics of the shape combine the characteristics to obtain the intended technical result, and are therefore attributable to the technical result. 3. In the present case, the shape of the CTM proprietors 3D trade mark is required to obtain a technical result (i.e. ‘heating), even if that result can be achieved by other shapes. 4. Indeed, the existence of an expired patent where the summary of the invention, together with the inventors claims, all describe features which are visible in the CTM proprietors trade mark strongly support the Boards view that the shape taken as a whole is functional (conduits placed above and below the firebox rather than on the sides; parallel and circular conduits; partial folding of the conduits around the firebox; semi circular aperture for fuel). 5. The ‘aesthetic features which the CTM proprietor ascribes to its trade mark, if they exist at all, are simply the outcome of good design considerations, but do not render the mark fanciful in any way. 6. In summary when the representation of the CTM proprietors trade mark is carefully examined, there is nothing distinctive remaining over and above its functionality which might allow it to act as a trade mark. 7. The contested decision is annulled and the CTM registration is declared invalid.

1. Article 7(1)(c) CTMR is not applicable in the case at hand.  The evidence clearly shows that ‘GAKIC is an invented expression.  While it is true that it is used for ‘a glycine and L-arginine salt of [alpha]-ketoisocaproic acid calcium this is not sufficient in itself to assert that the mark designates a characteristic of the goods.  It is correct, as claimed by the Cancellation applicant, that there is no need to prove that the term is already used in trade to apply Article 7(1)(c) CTMR but there must be concrete indications that it may be used in the future.  The Cancellation applicant failed to prove that. 2. The term is always used in capital letters and seems to designate a specific patented product. In a US Paten No. the patentee first gives the chemical name of the compound and then inserts the word ‘GAKIC in block capital letters and refers to ‘GAKIC in capital letters thereafter.  It is also referred to as ‘GAKIC treatment.  Furthermore, the inventors, i.e. researchers at the University of Florida, assigned that invention to the University, which became the patentee.  The University filed an application for ‘GAKIC as a trade mark in the USA shortly after the patent was granted on the basis of intent to use and obtained registration thereof on the strength of evidence of use.  The user was a sister company of the CTM proprietor, which was assigned the US trade mark from the patentee in 2005. 3. The evidence establises that the term is invariably associated with a specific patented product related to the CTM proprietor. There is no that third parties with no connection with the CTM proprietor have used the term ‘GAKIC in a generic sense. Furthermore, there is no indication on the market of other trade marks having the alleged generic compound 'gakic'. 4. The term is also capable of distinguishing the registered goods in terms of their origin. 5. The Cancellation applicants claim of bad faith is only based on the arguments that the mark is devoid of distinctiveness and should not have been registered.  Thus, it has not put forward any arguments and even less documented that the CTM proprietor should have acted contrary to accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices.  Consequently, this claim must be rejected. 6. The appeal is dismissed.

1.	An extract from the Australian Business Research (ABR) database indicates Mr. Urriola as current director of Astral Property Pty. Ltd. A certified document relating the ‘Registro Mercantil de Asturias (Commercial Register of Asturias) demonstrates that Mr. Urriola is furthermore ‘Administrador Único (managing director) of Atlantis Marketing Euro S.L. Although aforesaid documentation was adduced on appeal stage, the Board considers it admissible to the proceedings for the following reasons. Firstly, they act supportive towards evidence presented in the cancellation proceedings rather than being new evidence. Secondly, the cancellation applicant was not induced to submit further evidence that might substantiate that Mr. Urriola was empowered to act as a representative before the CD.2.	It can be left undecided whether the relations between the cancellation applicant and the CTM proprietor amounted to an agent-principal-relationship as required under Article 8(3) CTMR, taking into account that that required some sort of fiduciary relationship and in particular more than a mere distributorship. It can also be left undecided whether a claim under Article 8(3) CTMR can be based on the existence of an unregistered trade mark held by the alleged principal in a country which is not a Member State of the European Community. In the explanation of the grounds for the request for declaration of invalidity, the cancellation applicant, in the respective part related to Article 8(3) CTMR, merely set out that the CTM proprietor was its agent, with reference to the details of the sales of the cancellation applicants products. Therefore, this ground for declaration of invalidity is not even properly substantiated, and the request does not comply with Rule 37(b)(ii) CTMIR, also read in conjunction with Rule 15(2)(i) and Rule 19(2)(e) CTMIR.3.	In the form for a request for declaration of invalidity, on page 3 of the official form, relating to Article 8(4) CTMR), the cancellation applicant merely ticked the box ‘non-registered marks but did not indicate any Member State where such right should be protected. Moreover, the cancellation applicant did not indicate any provisions of national law or make any explanations as to the basis on which it would have been entitled to enjoin the use of a junior mark. The request is also in this regard in breach of Rule 37(b)(ii) CTMIR.4.	The cancellation applicant failed to submit any evidence of use of a mark prior to the filing date of the CTM. 5.	No use by the cancellation applicant or Atlantis of a sign ‘D-Raintank prior to the CTM filing date was shown, there was even no use shown on behalf of the cancellation applicant or Atlantis at all of the term ‘D-Raintank applied as a mark to the goods in issue in Class 19, and the CTM proprietors own use has to be disregarded. It cannot be emphasized too strongly that the cancellation applicants evidence amounts to arguing that it would be bad faith to use ones own trade mark. In that context, Mr. Urriolas declaration states that in Australia the cancellation applicant had used (only) the mark ‘MATRIX. Also, the cancellation applicant did not file any evidence showing that a mark ‘D-Raintank was affixed on the goods it sold, be it for export to Europe. This analysis is based exclusively on the cancellation applicants own evidence, without the need of relying on the evidence submitted by the CTM proprietor. As a result, the CTM proprietor cannot be accused of having tried to block somebody elses mark as the cancellation applicant did not prove that it had a mark at the material point in time, or in any manner used the sign ‘D-Raintank as a mark. Ad abundantiam, it follows from the examination of the ground of bad faith that the cancellation applicant was not able to prove use as its own mark in a Member State prior to the filing date of the CTM.6.	The Board annuls the contested decision and rejects the request for declaration of invalidity in its entirety.

results.summary: 1.	The signs ‘greendip and ‘Green Dip are almost identical, since they only differ in that there is a space between the words ‘Green and ‘Dip, beginning with a capital letter, in the earlier mark whereas the CTM application consists of one word in lower case.  Therefore the signs are visually highly similar. They are phonetically identical. Considering the high similarity of the signs and the identity of the goods ‘skin care products for dairy farms in Class 3 and ‘hygienic products for dairy farms in Class 5 against ‘preparations/agents for cleaning animal teats, there is a likelihood of confusion in Denmark and therefore the contested mark cannot be registered.2.	The appeal is dismissed.

1. The purpose of the UK proceedings was to determine whether TFLs registration of the trade mark BRUTT (and of other trade marks containing the word BRUTT) should be cancelled on the ground i.a. that TFL acted in bad faith at the time when it applied for the mark. 2. The purpose of the present proceedings is likewise to determine whether TFLs registration of the trade mark BRUTT should be cancelled on the ground i.a. that TFL acted in bad faith when it applied for the mark. 3. The same trade mark is involved, namely BRUTT. 4. The goods are also exactly the same. 5. The same filing date is involved because TFL, when filing the CTM application, claimed the priority of the UK application. 6. The concept of bad faith in both sets of proceedings is the same. 7. All those BRUTT UK registrations were declared invalid on 20 December 2006 by decision of Richard Arnold, QC, sitting as Appointed Person in an appeal against the decision of a hearing officer in the UK Trade Mark Registry. 8. The Appointed Person assumed, in his decision, that bad faith was a concept of Community law and referred to the practice of OHIM in relation to Article 51(1)(b) CTMR. 9. This confirms that the issue tried by the Appointed Person and the issue to be resolved in the present proceedings are one and the same. 10. In the Boards view, the res judicata doctrine bars the Board of Appeal from reopening the question of bad faith, since all the conditions laid down in the judgment in Case T-0162/94 ‘NMB France are met. 11. The Board could only refrain from applying the doctrine res judicata if the Appointed Person lacked jurisdiction or his decision was clearly unsound or unreasonable or the rules of natural justice milited against following his decision. 12. Which is not the case here. 13. Following its review of the case, the Board has no difficulty in endorsing the Appointed Persons finding that in filing the CTM applications, TFL did not observe the standards of acceptable commercial behaviour observed by reasonable and experienced businessmen. 14. The contested decision is annulled and CTM No 2015097 is declared invalid.

results.summary: 1.	 The burden of proof in relation to the use and/or reputation of the earlier trade marks invoked did not lie with the applicant.   2.	In the first place, neither of the applicant's trade marks had been registered for more than five years at the relevant dates.  Therefore, even if there had been an explicit application by the proprietor under this provision, there would have been no requirement to furnish proof of use of the earlier trade marks as provided for therein. 3.	Having taken account, in particular, of the (uncontested) identity of the conflicting goods ‘optical) spectacles, cases for spectacles, spectacle glass frames, lenses for spectacles, optical frames in Class 9, of the visual and aural similarities noted between the signs, as well as their conceptual identity/affinity, at least for a considerable portion of the English-speaking public of the Community, there is a likelihood that many English-speaking consumers (considering also that the latter take particular care when purchasing the articles in question) may be led, if not to confuse one trade mark with the other, to believe that they come from the same undertaking or from economically-linked undertakings.4.	The appeal is dismissed.

1. Both parties claim to be exclusively entitled to use the name GERSON as a trade mark for the provision of nutritional therapy and for the supply of various related goods. 2. After examining the evidence submitted on both sides the Board has concluded that neither of the parties can claim such an exclusive right because GERSON is not a trade mark at all for the relevant G&S but simply a descriptive term which refers to a nutritional therapy developed, practised and divulged by Dr Max Gerson. 3. As such, the term is in the public domain, along with the theoretical and practical knowledge divulged by Dr Gerson. 4. Therefore, the mark should not have been registered as a CTM in accordance with Article 7(1)(c) CTMR and the registration must be declared invalid pursuant to Articles 51(2), 55(1)(a) and 56(5) CTMR. 5. As regards the respondents ground of appeal under Article 51(1)(b), the CTM must also be declared invalid on the ground that the appellant was acting in bad faith when he filed the CTM application. 6. In the Boards view, the appellants primary aim in filing the application was to create a situation in which he alone could use the name GERSON in connection with Gerson therapy.  7. The appeal is dismissed.

Facts in brief
In the 1990, Elio Fiorucci (applicant for cancellation), an Italian designer of clothes, sold his ‘creative assets’ to Edwin Co. Ltd (proprietor of the CTM); for several years, the applicant for cancellation collaborated with the proprietor of the CTM. 
In the late 1990s, the proprietor of the CTM registered the CTM ‘ELIO FIORUCCI’ for goods in Classes 3, 18 and 25.
The applicant for cancellation filed a cancellation action based on Article 51(1)(c) CTMR [ex Article 50(1)(c)] and Article 53(2)(a) CTMR [ex Article 52(1)(a)]. 
The Cancellation Division cancelled the CTM since it infringed the rights of the applicant for cancellation to its own name pursuant to Article 53(2)(a) CTMR and Article 8(3) Italian Industrial Property Code [ex Article 21(3) Italian Trade Mark Law]. It did not give any ruling whether the CTM should be revoked in accordance with Article 51(1)(c) CTMR.
The Boards of Appeal, with decision R 0238/2005-1, allowed the appeal and annulled the decision of the Cancellation Division, holding that the ground of invalidity referred to in Article 53(2)(a) CTMR did not apply since it did not fall within the cases provided for under national law (Article 8(3) It. IPC). The request for revocation based on Article 51(1)(c) CTMR was also rejected.
Findings of the CFI
Revocation - misleading the public
In accordance with Article 51(1)(c) CTMR, a CTM shall be revoked, if in consequence of the use made of it by the proprietor the CTM is liable to mislead the public as, particularly as to the nature, quality or geographical origin. 
The CFI held, first of all, that the use of patronymic marks is a widespread practice in all sectors of commerce and the public concerned is well aware that, behind every patronymic mark, there is not necessarily a fashion designer of that name. However, the applicant for cancellation did not submit any evidence showing any use whatsoever of the CTM ‘ELIO FIORUCCI’ after its registration. In the absence of such evidence, there can be no question of use liable to mislead the public within the meaning of Article 51(1)(c) CTMR.
Relative ground of invalidity - right to a name
The CFI held that the interpretation of Article 8(3) It. IPC, which was based only on academic writing in absence of any jurisprudence, was incorrect, since it considered wrongly that, once the name of a well-known person was registered or used as a mark, its ‘migration’ to the trade mark sector was ‘implemented’. Consequently, the Boards assessment was wrong. 
Consequently, the contested decision was annulled. 
Alternative argument
The CFI did not give any ruling on the alternative argument presented by the Office and the proprietor of the CTM, that the applicant for cancellation included the right to its name as part of the ‘creative assets’ which were sold to the proprietor of the CTM since that would imply the exercise of administrative and investigatory functions specific to the Office and would therefore upset the institutional balance on which the division of jurisdiction between the Office and the Court is based. 
Result
The contested decision is annulled with respect to the relative ground of invalidity (right to a name); it is dismissed for the reminder (misleading use).
The Office is ordered to bear its own costs and two thirds of those incurred by the applicant for cancellation; the proprietor of the CTM is ordered to bear one third of the costs of applicant for cancellation.

results.summary: 1. the applicant claims for the first time on appeal that there are reasons to substantiate the lack of use of the earlier mark. 2. It argues, specifically, that it was because of the prior Spanish trade marks and the unexpected renewal thereof that it decided to halt plans to expand into Europe. 3. Irrespective of whether those assertions are admissible, the Board notes that the grounds adduced relate to the businesss commercial strategies, which neither prove genuine use nor substantiate lack of use. 4. Consequently, use of the earlier marks has not been proven and sound reasons for lack of use have not been given. 5. As to substantiation of the earlier marks reputation, the fact that there are a number of premises bearing the mark, that documentation concerning minimal advertising activity and sales figures have been provided and that references to the trade mark can be found by means of a Google search does not mean that the trade mark has a reputation in the countries indicated. 6. As regards the applicants claim that the proprietor acted in bad faith, the Board does not consider the proprietor to have acted in bad faith when requesting economic compensation for transferring the CTM considering market freedom and that purchase/sale is a private contract whereby parties might reach agreement as they deem appropriate. 7. An oral hearing would not be useful since the issue raised can be resolved on the basis of the detailed assertions made by the parties in their written submissions and the evidence filed. 8. The appeal is dismissed.

1. The burden to prove bad faith is on the cancellation applicant; good faith is presumed until the opposite is proven. 2. In none of the invoices submitted by the appellant  are the letters ‘FS mentioned in the part of the invoice specifying the item sold. On the letterhead of the invoices and as a complement to its trade name Feng Shen, as well as in catalogues, the appellant uses another figurative signs 'FS', which differs clearly from the CTM. This version of the sign is also the one which is registered on behalf of the appellant in China. 3. The appellant's interest in the trade mark only became ignited when the respondent terminated the business relationship. At that point in time the respondent was perfectly free to produce the zippers himself or let them be produced by another company. It was the appellant who sought to exert pressure on the respondent by invoking bad faith and trade mark rights in order to force the respondent to either continue the contractual business relationship with it or leave the business to it. 4. The respondent did not act in bad faith when he filed the CTMA and therefore the registration of the mark cannot be declared invalid. 5. The appeal is dismissed.

1. The refusal of the request for declaration of invalidity was based on the fact that the CTM contains a word element without any meaning in relation to ovens, which renders the mark distinctive. 2. The graphical representation as originally filed does not contain any description which would further clarify what the mark consistis of. The BoA has studied the images which were provided and found that it is impossible to see that the 3D shape of the oven contains anything on its side. The possible text included on the side of the oven is too unclear and imprecise to contribute anything to the distinctiveness of the 3D mark. 3. It is clear that the contested decision is based on an incorrect appreciation of the facts of the case. 4. Therefore the appeal is upheld and the contested decision is annulled. 5. Taking into account that the distinctiveness of the 3D mark as a whole has not been examined at all and that this element has also been taken into account when deciding on whether Article 51(1)(b) CTMR is applicable, the BoA remits the case back to the CD to examine whether Article 51(1)(a) and/or (b) CTMR are applicable to the case.

1.In the present case the shape of the applied-for volley ball is standard. It is round as a ball usually is and must be. The trade mark proprietor only argues that distinctiveness is conferred by the colours and their configuration and arrangement.2.The configuration of the ball is also not ‘decorative. It does not represent an aesthetic choice of the client, it will not be bought because consumers prefer this over another colour, and it is not decorative in the sense that it would decorate (render more attractive) the interior of a living room. Volley balls are not displayed as items of decoration. The ball as applied for may also be perceived as aesthetically pleasant but it is common ground that a sign may fulfil various functions so that the mere fact that the sign in issue has also other functions is not a ground for its refusal or invalidity, unless it is found that those other functions predominate in such a way that the public would no longer perceive the sign as a reference to a commercial origin of the goods3.As regards the colours, the representation as filed shows merely one possibility for combining various colours to a particular surface pattern out of an infinite number of possible patterns. Not only that there are three colours, their exact arrangement is complex. Verbally it would be futile to try to describe it. Their arrangement does not follow any perceivable geometric rule. The various panels themselves are not merely repeating basic geometric shapes. The arrangement and configuration is truly specific and complex enough to depart from a mere geometric pattern and straightforward enough to be memorizable.4.In the absence of any objective and verifiable reason to deny distinctiveness, the contested decision must be upheld and the Board concludes that the mark as registered is distinctive for ‘volley balls in such a manner and to such a degree as to enable the relevant average consumer to distinguish the commercial origin of the balls on the basis of the colour configuration and arrangement as depicted in the representation of the mark.5.The appellant wished to use the proprietors mark himself and feels barred from that by the CTM registration and the behaviour of the FIVB. Bad faith is out of question. The irrelevance to the FIVB rules, for which, by the way, the trade mark proprietor is not even responsible, has already been discussed. When the mark was filed by its proprietor, it was its own mark as it used it at that time. That is its genuine right.6.The appeal is dismissed.
