results.summary: 1. The earlier British trade mark cannot be the subject of the request for proof or use made by the CTM proprietor under Article 56(2) and (3) CTMR since it was registered less than five years prior to the publication of the application for a declaration of invalidity. 2. In any case, the request should have been made before the Cancellation Division and not at the appeal stage. 3. As regards the claim under Article 8(1)(b) the signs are globally similar. 4. In visual terms, the earlier mark is a figurative mark. 5. Owing to their size and appearance, COOL and POINT are the main components. 6. These words make up the CTM, which is purely a word mark. 7. The visual aspects that they share mean that the marks are pronounced in an identical manner. 8. They also have the same meaning in English. 9. Owing to the identity or similarity between the goods in the contested classes 9 and 11, there is likelihood of confusion. 10. The appeal is dismissed.

1.	Before the Opposition Division, the applicant asked the opponent to file proof of use of the earlir marks. The opponent filed various documents, which the applicant claimed were insufficient. The Opposition Division invited the opponent to comment on the applicants submissions, but expressly stated that it would not take into account any new evidence and arguments that the opponent might submit at that stage.2.	The Opposition Division rejected the opposition in its entirety for lack of proof of genuine use for all the opponents earlier trademarks. The opponent appealed the matter to the Boards of Appeal.3.	The Board allowed the appeal, finding that the evidence submitted before the Opposition Division (affidavits, invoices, price list and catalogues) showed genuine use of Austrian word mark No 63 756 on the Austrian market for ‘television apparatus, magnetic sound recording and reproducing apparatus and signalling apparatus in Class 9. The documents showed the nature and place of the use. The extent of use (sales for EUR 2 500 000) as shown in the affidavit was sufficient to show that the use served a real commercial purpose and was not merely token use. The fact that the documentation covered only two years was irrelevant. There is no requirement that use has to be proven as regards the whole five-year period. It is only required that genuine use has been proven at any time during the relevant period. The Board also noted that the opponent was not required to submit all invoices in relation to its sales, but only samples, showing a minimum level of sales.4.	The Board found that use for other goods had not been proved, inter alia because the relevant documents were not dated and the products shown in them did not correspond to the products mentioned in the relevant affidavit.5.	While the Board accepted the additional evidence submitted by the opponent before the Board of Appeal as complementary evidence and took it into account, it did not lead to use being proved for more products than the ones for which it had already been proved. The only relevant document was not sufficiently dated.6.	The Board also found that the Opposition Division had not given the opponent sufficient opportunity to file evidence of use, thereby committing a substantial procedural violation. Inviting the opponent to comment on the applicants submissions which deny that use has been proved, but stating at the same time that the Office will not take into account any new evidence or arguments submitted by the opponent at that stage contravenes Article 43(1) CTMR.The appeal fee is reimbursed.. 7.	The case is remitted back to the Opposition Division. Each party bears its own costs incurred in the appeal. The appeal fee is reimbursed. The final decision on the costs of the opposition proceedings falls in the competence of the Opposition Division following its assessment of the merits of the case.

keywords: Identity of the goods and services
results.summary: 1. The contested CTM is identical to the earlier mark and it covers identical goods.  These findings have not been challenged in the appeal.  The request was, therefore, rightly allowed by the Cancellation Division.  2. The proceedings relating to validity of the CTM proprietors Spanish registrations are obviously irrelevant for the validity and scope of protection of the cancellation applicants French registrations.  Therefore, there are no grounds to suspend the proceedings in the present case. 3. The CTM proprietors submissions alleging the cancellation applicants bad faith are unsubstantiated and can, therefore, not be considered well-founded. 4. The appeal is dismissed.

1.It is disingenuous to claim that the mark is ‘untraditional or ‘imaginative. A quick glance at the exhibits of other ‘shopping cart icons produced by the applicant itself shows that it sits somewhere in the range of identical and similar devices found in Internet shopping. Far from demonstrating that its trade mark diverges from the norm in the field, many of the examples of ‘add to cart buttons which the applicant adduces actually confirm that such ‘shopping cart icons are the rule. The majority of examples, although lacking a figure of a cart, nevertheless feature words such as ‘add to cart, ‘add to shopping cart, or ‘your cart. In other words, either in the form of a device, or in words, or in combinations of the two, the concept of a ‘shopping cart is almost ubiquitous in the trade of Internet shopping.2.It is evident that although the applicants shopping cart design is not formally identical in every respect to the examples cited by the applicant itself, it does not significantly diverge from many of the figurative examples and is conceptually similar to the verbal equivalent e.g. ‘add to shopping cart. 3.The applicant refers to the combination of elements which makes up its mark and that the shopping cart icon is not the most distinctive part. However, apart from the shopping cart, the remaining so-called ‘elements are little more than banal figures - a circle and an oblong. Even when the mark is considered as a whole, these elements in combination with the shopping cart are not capable of generating any degree of distinctive character.4.The applicant requests the reimbursement of the appeal fee, claiming that the Office committed a substantial procedural violation by not allowing it to submit its observations on the rejection of the mark for ‘advertising; business management; business administration; office functions. However, since in this case the appeal is not successful, the request must be denied. 5.The Board dismisses the appeal and confirms that the scope of refusal of the CTM application laid out in the contested decision did not include ‘Advertising; business management; business administration; office functions in Class 35 and that therefore the mark can proceed for these services.

results.summary: 1. The term ‘16PF refers as regards the substantial portion of Community consumers, to a specific test authored by the founder of the respondent. However, no theory known as ‘16PF exists. 2. Not sufficient evidence was filed to show that the term will be understood by the relevant customers as an indication of the kind of goods or services offered by the appellant or third party. Thus, the term is capable of distinguishing registered goods and services in terms of their origin. 3. The letters and a digit applied for, considered individually, or together, are meaningless. The evidence clearly shows that ‘16PF is an invented combination. No characteristics of the of goods and services are described by ‘16PF. 4. The questionnaire is invariably associated with its inventor, R. Cattell. Therefore, this term has not, for a substantial part of the relevant public in the Community, become customary as an equivalent of any type of personality questionnaire. 5. Registration of the term does not prevent third parties from such use. The registration does not monopolise a psychological theory, because there is no such theory named ‘16PF, nor does it have any impact upon psychological research. 6. It follows that the appeal must be dismissed.

1. The applicant for revocation does not have to show a special legal interest in its request. 2. Documents submitted during the appeal proceedings are inadmissible, since the owner of the CTM had apple opportunities to submit proof of use.3. The documents submitted before the Cancellation Division do not show genuine use during the last five years before filing the request for revocation. 4. Possible license agreements irrelevant, since the owner did not specified for which goods and services such agreements exist; additionally, there is no that licensee actually used the trade mark within the EU. The use of the trade mark on toys is irrelevant, since it cannot be attributed to the owner; furthermore, no sales figures etc were submitted. 5. The use of the trade mark in the USA is not use in EU; whether flights were booked from Europe by Europeans is not relevant for the case. 6. The use of the trade mark in movies etc. is not use for goods and services, and furthermore not trade mark use at all. 7. Notoriously known trade mark needs also use. 8. An agreement between USA and Germany irrelevant, since CTM. 9. The CTM is revoked with date 23/09/2005.

results.summary: The appeal is well founded for the same reasons set out in detail in R 0633/2007-2 to which the Board refers to avoid unnecessary repetition.

1. Article 51(1)(b) CTMR does not allow applications for registration of trade marks to be made for abusive or fraudulent ends which are contrary to the objectives pursued by Community trade mark law. In such a hypothesis the applicants purpose is not to protect a trade mark according to its distinctive function but to wield the registration as a weapon or tool to reach an abusive or fraudulent end deviated from the legitimate functions of trade marks, the protection of which are the objectives pursued by the Community trade mark law.2. It is a general principle of law that good faith is presumed until the contrary is proved. 3. Bad faith can be considered as meaning ‘dishonesty which would fall short of the standards of acceptable commercial behaviour.  There is bad faith when the CTM applicant intends, through registration, to lay its hands on the trade mark of a third party with whom it had contractual or pre-contractual relations, or, any kind of relation where good faith applies and imposes on the applicant the duty of fair play in relation to the legitimate interests and expectations of the other party with regard to the trade mark at issue. 4. In the present case this has not happened, since there was no relevant relationship between the CTM proprietor and the Cancellation applicant before or at the time of filing the contested CTM. Moreover the Cancellation applicants interests with regard to the trade mark at issue cannot be regarded as legitimate.5. The mere creation or adoption of a name is not a legal source of trade mark rights.  Anyone who adopts a trade mark without taking the precaution of getting it registered runs the risk of seeing it registered by a third party on a first-come-first-served basis.6. Mere preparations for trading are insufficient to generate goodwill.7. The appeal is upheld and the contested decision annulled.

results.summary: 1. The BoA confirms that not only bad faith has not been proven but the contrary can be implied from the circumstances of the case. The BoA will dismiss the appeal based on the consideration that the circumstances of the case point to the conclusion that the appellant acquiesced to the appropriation by the respondent of the trade mark (and the trade name) MURINA in the EC. 2. It is most unlikely, in the circumstances of the case,that the appellant ignored that LUXFACTOR had applied for and acquired a MURINA CTM in the EU given their ongoing business relationships. 3. Concerning the issue of whether a legitimate agent or representative relationship existed or not, the long years elapsed until the filing of the invalidity action plead in favour of the existence of a consent; so there is no need to discuss such issue.4. Therefore, the appellant has failed to convince the Office that the original proprietor of the attacked CTM acted in bad faith, or was the appellants agent at the point in time when the application for the attacked CTM was filed.5. The appeal is dismissed.

1.As regards bad faith: As a general principle the good faith is presumed until the contrary is proved, the burden of proof is on the cancellation applicant. There is bad faith when the CTM applicant intends through registration to lay its hands on the trade mark of a third party with whom it had contractual or pre-contractual relations, or otherwise infringes the legitimate interests and expectations of the other party. 2. In the present case there was no relationship between the parties and consequently the former had no bona fide duty to the latter at the time of filing the application. Once the CTM proprietor terminated all contractual relationships with the cancellation applicant was able to file the application for CTM. Moreover, the only user of the trade mark has been the CTM proprietor. The cancellation applicant could not invoke any trade mark right. 3. Consequently there is no evidence that the CTM proprietor acted in bad faith in filing the contested CTM. 4. As regards the substantiation of the artists name and the commercial designation: The cancellation applicant has not substantiated the rights to the name in the Netherlands nor the right to prohibit the use of the contested CTM in that country as required. Furthermore, the additional relative grounds for invalidity based on the rights to the contested name in Germany are inadmissible because they were submitted after the invalidity request had been filed. 5. Consequently the appeal is dismissed.

1. The meaning of ‘SMETANOVÝ, or ‘creamy, directly describes the goods referred to (Class 29). Therefore, the trade mark is only descriptive in part of the EU, namely the Czech Republic. It is clear that, in the present case, the appellant is not in breach of any rules or standards of the legislation applicable when the application in question was filed. Even though the contested trade mark is descriptive in Czech, the circumstances of the present dispute, this descriptive character cannot justify - on its own - the cancellation of the trade mark in question. 2. Therefore, the allegation of bad faith cannot be accepted. The respondent has failed to show the existence either of a fraudulent or dishonest application or of business conduct, by the proprietor, that any trader would consider unacceptable.3. Furthermore, if the descriptive character of the contested sign were an obstacle to competitors in the sense that use of the trade mark represents a monopoly allowed over a term used in everyday language that is needed to describe the intrinsic qualities of the goods in Class 29, it would be possible to bring an action based on Art. 106(2) CTMR in the Czech Republic.4. The appeal is upheld and the application for a declaration of invalidity dismissed.

1. Bad faith may be defined as referring to the state of someone, who knowingly by doing something contrary to accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices, gains an unjust advantage or causes unjust damage to others. 2. It is a general principle of law that good faith is presumed. As a result, it is for the party who denies the existence thereof to provide the relevant facts and to prove that it did not exist. As evidence of bad faith, the cancellation applicant refers exclusively to certain clauses in the three agreements in question and has provided additionally, only an extract from Kerlys Law of Trade Marks and Trade Names on the subject of interlocutory applications for an interim injunction. 3. The Board does not consider that the cancellation applicant has met the onus of showing that in filing the application for the CTM he has acted in bad faith. 4. Firstly, it has to be noted from the evidence, the brochures, the extracts from the annual reports and examples in the 2000 Settlement Agreement provided that the Johnson Pump name and logo are clearly the house mark of the Johnson Pump Group Sweden, which the JP Group Sweden has used for many years prior to the CTM application. 5. Secondly, a breach of contract does not necessarily constitute an act of bad faith. 6. Thirdly, even if it were to constitute an act of bad faith, no breach of contract has ever been found by an English court. The Cancellation Division has no competence to access the existence of a breach of contract given that the agreements in question contained a clause about the exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts. 7. The appeal is dismissed.

keywords: Sign used in the course of trade
results.summary: 1. The concept of bad faith may apply to whoever is aware that what he or she is doing is not correct, because it is in conflict, for example, with ethical standards or standards of commercial equity. Additionally, the behaviour must result in unjust prejudice to others. The bad faith of the person performing the action should not be measured subjectively, but objectively, on the basis of the information available to the person when performing the action. Bad faith must proven at the moment of filing a trade mark.2. If one person believes to have a right of ownership in an asset, and a third person is also claiming ownership, none of them is acting in bad faith. 3. Cancellation proceedings are not intended to deal with the question who is proprietor of a trade mark. Such proceedings must be made in front of the competent national authorities. 4. A person convinced of being the inventor of a trade mark cannot act in bad faith at the moment of filing said trade mark. 5. When basing its opposition on a well known trade mark (Article 6bis PCU) the opponent has to show that the public attributes such trade mark to itself and owner of the opposed trade mark. The same applies with respect to a sign used in the course of trade.

keywords: Sign used in the course of trade
results.summary: 1. On the face of the evidence, everything seems to point towards the fact that Messrs Edwards and Dent were the genuine ‘inventors of the mark back in 1993. 2. The existence of which the parties agree, is the transfer of the TRAXDATA marks in 1997 and 1998 to other entities controlled by Messrs Edwards and Dent. Later, the same marks are transferred to Ritrax Corporation (who owns the four CTMs now), controlled by Ritek, a Taiwanese manufacturer of recordable CDs, which has been supplying Traxdata France with these products.3. The Board could not find that those who masterminded the four CTM applications were aware, on the respective filing dates, that the TRAXDATA mark was not their exclusive property. All the evidence points in the opposite direction: Messrs Edwards and Dent filed (in the name of different entities they controlled) the CTM applications honestly and genuinely believing that they were protecting their own mark, on which they honestly and genuinely believed no other party could validly claim proprietary rights. There is no trace of any documents whereby Traxdata France has ever been recognised as having proprietary rights in the mark, either from the outset or transferred later. Therefore, those who inspired the CTM applications acted in good faith at the time of filing.4. In addition in none of the four cases is there evidence that Messrs Edwards and Dent (who directed the filings) were conscious that they were filing trade marks belonging to others. 5. Moreover, if Traxdata France believes that its legitimate business expectations have been violated or that the termination of the licence agreement was incorrect, it should not apply for the cancellation of the mark but sue Ritrax before the Courts.6. Thus, the Board does not see any risk of confusion on the French market if the CTMs are used there. 7. The appeal is dismissed.

If one person believes to have a right of ownership in an asset, and a third person is also claiming ownership, none of them is acting in bad faith. 3. Cancellation proceedings are not intended to deal with the question who is proprietor of a trade mark. Such proceedings must be made in front of the competent national authorities. 4. A person convinced of being the inventor of a trade mark cannot act in bad faith at the moment of filing said trade mark. 5. When basing its opposition on a well known trade mark (Article 6bis PCU) the opponent has to show that the public attributes such trade mark to itself and owner of the opposed trade mark. The same applies with respect to a sign used in the course of trade.

keywords: Sign used in the course of trade
results.summary: 1. On the face of the evidence, everything seems to point towards the fact that Messrs Edwards and Dent were the genuine ‘inventors of the mark back in 1993. 2. The existence of which the parties agree, is the transfer of the TRAXDATA marks in 1997 and 1998 to other entities controlled by Messrs Edwards and Dent. Later, the same marks are transferred to Ritrax Corporation (who owns the four CTMs now), controlled by Ritek, a Taiwanese manufacturer of recordable CDs, which has been supplying Traxdata France with these products.3. The Board could not find that those who masterminded the four CTM applications were aware, on the respective filing dates, that the TRAXDATA mark was not their exclusive property. All the evidence points in the opposite direction: Messrs Edwards and Dent filed (in the name of different entities they controlled) the CTM applications honestly and genuinely believing that they were protecting their own mark, on which they honestly and genuinely believed no other party could validly claim proprietary rights. There is no trace of any documents whereby Traxdata France has ever been recognised as having proprietary rights in the mark, either from the outset or transferred later. Therefore, those who inspired the CTM applications acted in good faith at the time of filing.4. In addition in none of the four cases is there evidence that Messrs Edwards and Dent (who directed the filings) were conscious that they were filing trade marks belonging to others. 5. Moreover, if Traxdata France believes that its legitimate business expectations have been violated or that the termination of the licence agreement was incorrect, it should not apply for the cancellation of the mark but sue Ritrax before the Courts.6. Thus, the Board does not see any risk of confusion on the French market if the CTMs are used there. 7. The appeal is dismissed.

1. It is clear from the file that the parties are co-owner of the Argentinean trade mark; however, it has not been evidenced under which terms the co-ownership was established and under what conditions the parties could use their trade marks. Consequently, the Board cannot come to any conclusion in favour of the cancellation applicants claim as far as the co-ownership on an international level is concerned and cannot conclude that the (minority) co-owner breached an agreement on the co-ownership in bad faith. 2. Negotiations between the co-owners and a third company to register the Argentinean trade mark as CTM cannot proof bad faith of the third company. 3. The appeal is dismissed.

1. Evidence, submitted together with the statement of grounds, and which completes and confirms the earlier filed evidence shall be admissible.2. The concept of ‘bad faith as mentioned in Article 52(1)(b) CTMR does not require the exists of an earlier right.  In is sufficient that the applicant for cancellation is the owner of a sign used in commerce. 3. The cancellation applicant has used the sign ‘ER in a large number of countries in and outside the European Union and that the CTM proprietor has been well aware of this use.  4. Hence, the challenged CTM should be invalidated in accordance with Article 51(1)(b) CTMR. 5. No need to deal with the other grounds for cancellation.

1. Mr. Y, the trade mark applicant at the Office, acted in his own name. An internal arrangement for the mark to be transferred to Mr. X does not make Mr. Y a mere representative of X and does not allow for the conclusion that the latter was the true CTM applicant.2. The BoA performs a contextual and teleological interpretation and concludes that the term applicant in this article has to be construed as meaning: a) the person applying for the CTM in his own name; b) the principal of an agent acting in the name of his principal; c) any person instructing a nominee to act in his (the nominees) own name, but who, according to an arrangement between them, merely serves the interests of the former, while acting in good faith and being unaware of the formers bad faith.3. Y neither mentioned an interest in this application nor did he claim that he had any say in the pursuit of the application. His only ‘incentive was the instruction given by X. Y was meant to assign the mark to X.4. X planned to open a restaurant business under the trade mark in the UK and other Member States. With respect to bad faith, this notion concerns unfair practices involving a lack of honest intention on the part of the CTM applicant at the time of filing, inter alia, when intending to ‘lay his hands on the trade mark of a third party with whom he had had contractual or pre-contractual relations. Whether or not an earlier relationship between the holder of a right and CTM applicant qualifies as sufficient for bad faith has to be determined on a case by case basis.5. The essential question is whether the relationship created a close enough business link between the parties to suggest that, given the information the true CTM applicant (X) had received in those discussions, it would be fair to expect him not to file an identical CTM application independently without prior information of the rights holder and a sufficiently long period of time for him to take any action against the CTM application that he considered appropriate. Art. 51(1)(b) does not seek to provide additional protection to that already granted by Art. 8 CTMR, namely Art. 8(2)(c). Holders of rights are protected against unfair practices even when they do not enjoy trade mark protection in the EU.6. The appeal is dismissed.

1. The arguments of the CTM proprietor regarding the discrimination of the CD regarding the validity of national trade marks in comparison with the powers of CTM courts regarding the validity of CTMs, amounts to the plea of illegality of the CTMR. Neither the CD, nor the BoA, however, have the competency to ignore or to alter the CTMR because the provisions are unlawful.2. The CD was correct in presuming that earlier national trade marks have full legal value as a basis for requesting the invalidity of a CTM, unless they have ceased to have effect. Moreover, the CD is not entitled to declare the revocation or invalidity of any national trade mark on the basis of an earlier well-known unregistered trade mark or on the basis of a bad faith application, nor to treat the trade marks as if they were invalid for such reasons3. There is no reason to reconsider the CDs assessment of the proof of use filed for the Swedish trade mark or the examination of likelihood of confusion. Although the CTM proprietor made the general request to annul the contested decision, the obligatory grounds of appeal did not contain any arguments as to why the contested decision was not to be followed.The appeal is dismissed.

Trade mark registration confers exclusive rights to protect the mark’s essential function of indicating the commercial origin of goods or services. A registration that seeks to restrict competition or gain rights for purposes beyond this function is inconsistent with the principles of honest practices and fair competition (§ 35). Application for registration of a trade mark is liable to be regarded as having been filed in bad faith notwithstanding the fact that, at the time of that application, there was no use by a third party on the internal market of an identical or similar sign for identical or similar goods or services (§ 37).
